title: Making Pipelines B and C Legible
text:
Preface
Matt — thank you for the careful read of the proposal and for the five comments you left in the margin. They land on exactly the right places. This short book is my answer. Its only goal is to make Pipeline B and especially Pipeline C legible: to show, from four independent angles, that they do what the proposal claims, and in particular to answer your central question — where does the external total variance \(T_{\mathrm{MI}}\) come from, and does it secretly require fitting the model many times?
The one-sentence answer, which the rest of the book unpacks, is:

title: Making Pipelines B and C Legible
text:
How this book is organized
Each chapter is short and aimed at one question. The book answers from four angles in turn:

Mathematics — the identity that licenses the point estimate, and the exact origin of the variance target (Part II).
Code — the actual functions that implement each operator, mapped one-to-one to the math (Part III).
A controlled simulation — synthetic data with a known ground truth, where we watch Pipeline C recover the truth and reproduce Pipeline A (Part IV).
PISA 2022 — the three pipelines run side by side on the real data, USA and Korea (Part V).

title: Making Pipelines B and C Legible
text:
A note on scope
This is a demonstration, not the manuscript. The simulations are deliberately at a smoke / pilot scale — small enough to run quickly and transparently, large enough to make the point. The aim is clarity and correctness, not the final production numbers. Where a claim is exact, we prove it; where it is approximate, we say so and show the diagnostic that flags it.
— JoonHo

title: 1  The Three Pipelines in One Picture
text:
1.1 The shared spine
PISA 2022 does not release a single achievement score per student. It releases \(M = 10\) plausible values (PVs) — ten draws from each student’s posterior ability distribution, generated by the consortium’s IRT model before the data file ever reaches you — so that a downstream analyst can propagate measurement uncertainty into any statistic. For a survey-weighted two-level random-intercept regression (an Allison hybrid with within-school slopes \(\beta^W\), between-school slopes \(\beta^B\), school effects \(\zeta_j\), and PISA’s 80 BRR-Fay replicate weights), the orthodox recipe is exactly what you would expect: fit the model ten times, once per PV, then pool the ten coefficient vectors and their variances by Rubin’s (1987) rules. That is Pipeline A — correct, defensible, and the reference against which everything in this book is judged.
The proposal is that you can obtain the same Rubin-style report — the same point estimate and the same total variance — from one MCMC run instead of ten. That is the claim of Pipelines B and C. Before any theorems, I want you to see why all three procedures are variations on a single skeleton, differing on only two axes.
The three pipelines have far more in common than not. They share a point center, a variance target, and a reshaping operator.
A single point center. Every pipeline reports the Rubin-pooled fixed-effect estimate \[
\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}} = \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\hat\beta_m,
\qquad
\hat\beta_m = (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega\, y^{(m)},
\] where \(\hat\beta_m\) is the per-PV GLS estimate and \(\Omega = V^{-1}\) is the marginal precision at plug-in variance components.
A single variance target. Every pipeline reports the Rubin total variance \[
T_{\text{MI}} = \bar U + \Bigl(1 + \tfrac{1}{M}\Bigr) B,
\] where \(\bar U = M^{-1}\sum_m \hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\) is the average within-PV design variance (assembled from the \(R = 80\) replicate weights with Fay shrinkage \(k = 0.5\)) and \(B = (M-1)^{-1}\sum_m(\hat\beta_m - \bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}})(\hat\beta_m - \bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}})^\top\) is the between-PV parameter scatter. Exactly how \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is assembled — and why it is the crux of your question — is Section 5.1.
A single reshaping operator. Raw MCMC draws have the right center but the wrong covariance: the sampler’s implied uncertainty, not PISA’s design-based uncertainty. The Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC) maps a raw draw cloud to a prescribed mean \(c\) and covariance \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\): \[
\beta_s^{\text{cal}} = c + L_{\text{target}}\,L_{\text{raw}}^{-1}\bigl(\beta_s - \bar\beta^{\text{raw}}\bigr),
\qquad A = L_{\text{target}}L_{\text{raw}}^{-1},
\] with \(L_{\text{raw}}, L_{\text{target}}\) the lower-triangular Cholesky factors of the raw and target covariances. CCC is deterministic and closed-form — it adds no sampling noise — and it acts only on the fixed-effect block \(\beta_{\text{FE}} = (\beta^W,\beta^B)^\top\); variance components pass through untouched. The reason, which turns out to be the heart of your “why only the conditional version” question, is that GLS fixed-effect gradients are linear in \(y^{(m)}\) while variance-component scores are not (Section 6.1).

title: 1  The Three Pipelines in One Picture
text:
1.2 The two axes of divergence
Given that spine, the three pipelines differ on exactly two choices — and nothing else.
Axis 1 — where the raw draws come from. Pipeline A runs \(M = 10\) independent MCMC chains, one per PV. Pipelines B and C share a single MCMC run from the stacked fractional bridge \[
\log q_{\text{SWL}}(\psi) = \log p(\psi) + \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\log L_m^0(\psi) + C,
\] a geometric-mean blend of the ten per-PV likelihoods. Theorem 2.2 (Chapter 3) shows that the mode of \(q_{\text{SWL}}\) over \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) equals \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) exactly — under the R3a plug-in regime, where a single pilot \(\hat V\) is held fixed. The real joint MCMC operates under R3b (variance components sampled jointly), which opens a small gap we measure rather than assume away (Chapters 3 and 10).
Axis 2 — at what granularity CCC is applied. Pipelines A and B call CCC \(M\) times, once per PV, each call targeting the per-PV BRR-Fay sandwich \(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\); the pooled \(T_{\text{MI}}\) then emerges afterward as the Rubin combination of those calibrated clouds. Pipeline C-Direct calls CCC exactly once, with center \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) and target \(T_{\text{MI}}\) supplied directly. The one-shot shortcut is licensed by Theorem 2.2: because the stacked bridge already centers its draws at \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\), a single CCC pass against \(T_{\text{MI}}\) reproduces what the ten-call-then-pool procedure produces.
One caution the picture makes visible: injecting \(T_{\text{MI}}\) as a per-PV target in A or B would be wrong — it would over-disperse each individual cloud by the between-PV term \((1+1/M)B\). The pooled target is a valid direct CCC input only for C-Direct’s single cloud.

Theorem 2.2’s mode identity — the raw stacked posterior mean equalling \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — is exact only under the R3a plug-in regime (a single pilot \(\hat V\) held fixed); the deployed R3b MCMC (variance components sampled jointly) opens a small gap between that raw mean and \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\). That gap never enters C-Direct’s report, which is centered on the external \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — so its point estimate equals Pipeline A’s exactly, by construction. We measure the raw-mean gap only as a verification diagnostic — see Chapter 3 and the known-truth simulation in Part IV.



Two things the table hides. First, Pipeline B is not simply Pipeline A with a gate bolted on: it reweights the one shared draw cloud toward each per-PV posterior using PSIS importance weights, and only then calibrates. “PSIS needed” means each of the ten per-PV weight sets is checked by a Pareto \(\hat k\) diagnostic; any \(\hat k \ge 0.7\) triggers a fallback (to Pipeline A for that PV, or to C-Direct) — this is exactly the tail concern you raised, and Section 7.1 develops it. Second, and I want to be explicit: Pipeline C does not escape the BRR-Fay cost.

title: 1  The Three Pipelines in One Picture
text:
1.5 Where the cost actually lives
If Pipeline C needs only one MCMC run, where did the work go? It went into the variance target — and that work is shared by all three pipelines. Assembling \(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\) and \(T_{\text{MI}}\) requires \(M(R+1) = 10 \times 81 = 810\) refits per country: one full-weight fit and \(R = 80\) replicate-weight fits for each of the ten PVs. Crucially these are fast lme4 REML fits, not MCMC — measured at roughly \(0.01\)–\(0.05\) s each at PISA scale (Section 5.1), so seconds to a few minutes per country (a pessimistic upper bound of $$15–30 minutes with a rich covariate set), against the roughly two-to-four hours that ten full MCMC fits demand (USA to Korea, measured at PISA scale in Section 13.1).
So Pipeline C does not make the variance target free, and it does not make it pipeline-specific: the 810 fits are model-specific and unavoidable, and every pipeline pays exactly the same price for them. What Pipeline C changes is one number only — the MCMC count, from ten to one. That is the whole of the efficiency claim, stated honestly. Your comment C4 asks where this target comes from and whether it secretly needs many model fits; the honest answer is “yes, many — but cheap ones, and shared,” and Section 5.1 makes it concrete.

title: 1  The Three Pipelines in One Picture
text:
1.6 What this book does
The remaining chapters develop this single picture from four angles:

Mathematics — the stacked MLE identity (Theorem 2.2) that licenses the point estimate; the translation from plausible-value variance to parameter variance; the BRR-Fay assembly of \(T_{\text{MI}}\); and the CCC algebra, including why it touches only \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\).
Code — five R functions in one-to-one correspondence with the five objects in the diagram above (stack_fractional_posterior, assemble_pisa_brr_fay_target, rubin_pool_matrix, ccc_twolevel, and the pipeline runners).
A known-truth simulation — synthetic PVs with a known generating \(\beta\), where we can watch Pipeline C recover the truth and reproduce Pipeline A to within Monte-Carlo error.
PISA 2022 — the three pipelines run side by side for the USA and Korea.

The next chapter restates your five comments verbatim and points each one to the chapter that answers it (and Section 14.1 closes them out at the very end). If you want to go straight to “where does \(T_{\text{MI}}\) come from without running A or B,” that is Section 5.1; if you want the stacked MCMC and the importance-sampling step, that is Section 7.1. This chapter’s only job was to put all three pipelines in one frame so the arguments that follow have somewhere to land.

title: 2  Your Questions, Restated — and a Roadmap
text:
2.1 The central one: where does the variance target come from? (C2, C4)
You left five comments on the proposal and one summarizing paragraph in your email. They are the best possible table of contents for this book, because each one points at a place where the proposal was too terse. This chapter quotes them back to you verbatim, says what I now understand each to be asking, and names the chapter that answers it. Nothing here is an answer yet — it is a map.
Your email put the central difficulty plainly:
That single paragraph actually contains two distinct questions — where does \(T_{\text{MI}}\) come from? and how does a variance over plausible values become a variance over \(\beta\)? — and they are answered in two different chapters. Let me take all five margin comments in the order that builds.
This is the heart of the matter, and your instinct is correct: \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is model-specific, and it does require fitting many models. The proposal hid that, which is what made Pipeline C look like it was conjuring a variance from nowhere. The clarification is about which kind of fit. The expensive object in this whole problem is the MCMC: Pipeline A runs ten of them, Pipeline C runs one. The variance target is a separate object, assembled from \(M(R{+}1) = 810\) fast lme4 REML replicate refits — seconds each, not hours — and that assembly is identical and shared across Pipelines A, B, and C. So \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is not gotten “without A or B”; it is gotten by the same BRR-Fay machinery all three share, which is cheap and has nothing to do with how many MCMC runs you did. Pipeline C’s only economy is the MCMC count. This is Section 5.1, the chapter I most want you to read.

section: 2.2 The connecting one: plausible-value variance vs. parameter variance (C3)
text:
2.2 The connecting one: plausible-value variance vs. parameter variance (C3)

C3. Isn’t this for the plausible values \(y\) not the model parameters (betas?) How do we connect this to the variance of the parameters?

Exactly the right question, and the proposal never drew the arrow. The between-imputation term \((1+1/M)B\) is not a variance of the plausible values \(y\). Each plausible value \(y^{(m)}\) is pushed through the regression to a coefficient vector \(\hat\beta_m = (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega\,
y^{(m)}\), and \(B\) is the scatter of those ten coefficient vectors in parameter space. The variability lives in \(y\) but is measured in \(\beta\), through the linear GLS map. Section 4.1 draws that arrow carefully, with the worked arithmetic.
crumbs: 2  Your Questions, Restated — and a Roadmap

title: 2  Your Questions, Restated — and a Roadmap
section: 2.3 The subtle one: why calibrate only the betas? (C5)
text:
2.3 The subtle one: why calibrate only the betas? (C5)

C5. fixing the variance components? why not do importance resampling for the joint distribution instead of the conditional/profile version?

Two good ideas are bundled here. First, why is the calibration restricted to the fixed effects, holding the variance components fixed? Because the licensing identity (Theorem 2.2) is exact only for \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\): the GLS fixed-effect gradient is linear in \(y^{(m)}\), while the variance-component scores are not, so the clean affine reshape has no analogue there — and the positivity constraint on \(\sigma^2\) would be violated by an affine map anyway. Second, why not importance-resample the joint distribution instead? You can — and that is exactly what Pipeline B does for the fixed-effect block, via PSIS. So C5 is not really an objection to Pipeline C so much as a description of the B-vs-C trade-off. Section 6.1 handles the “why only betas” half; Section 7.1 handles the “joint resampling” half. Calibrating the variance components themselves is a genuinely separate problem, and we defer it to the follow-up paper.
crumbs: 2  Your Questions, Restated — and a Roadmap

title: 2  Your Questions, Restated — and a Roadmap
section: 2.4 The generative one: making importance sampling work (C1)
text:
2.4 The generative one: making importance sampling work (C1)

C1. This seems like a good starting point, esp for the point estimates. For importance sampling we may want to further fit a second time and further temper to get better proposal values that are more likely to include the modes from the replicate distributions. Should double check the pareto version of importance sampling.

And from your email:

I think we might need higher multiples of \(M\) (say \(1/2M\) or \(1/3M\)) to flatten out the proposal distribution for the importance sampling. Each of the \(M\) target distributions could be shifted away from the overall mode. With larger sample sizes, the mode could be islands with less overlap … But too much tempering could skew the point estimates, hence wanting to run it for the \(1/M\) first.

This is not a question I need to defend against — it is a design idea I want to build with you. The stacked bridge is a good point-estimate proposal (Theorem 2.2 makes its mode exact), but it can be a poor importance-sampling proposal when the per-PV posteriors separate into the “islands” you describe. The Pareto \(\hat k\) diagnostic is our primary instrument for detecting this, PV by PV — and, taking your “double check the pareto version” seriously, whether it is sufficient once the modes separate is something we test in Section 11.1, not something we assume. Your tempering suggestion — raising the exponent to \(1/(2M)\) or \(1/(3M)\) to flatten the proposal — is a real fix, already reachable through one knob in the code. Section 7.1 lays out the geometry, and Section 11.1 actually runs your \(1/M\) vs \(1/2M\) vs \(1/3M\) experiment on data with a known answer, so we can see the trade-off you anticipated between proposal coverage and point-estimate stability.

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
text:
3.1 Setup
This chapter does one thing: it proves that running one MCMC on the stacked fractional bridge and reading off the posterior mean over the fixed effects yields exactly the Rubin-pooled point estimate \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — where, as I will be careful to define, \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) is the pooled estimate at a common plug-in variance. That algebraic fact — Theorem 2.2 — is the license for Pipeline C’s point estimate. It says nothing, by itself, about variance: the raw spread of the stacked posterior is not the right uncertainty, and correcting it is the job of Section 5.1 and Section 6.1. Here we nail only the center.
crumbs: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
text:
3.2 The two-level hybrid model
Students \(i\) are nested in schools \(j\). For plausible value \(m\), the Allison hybrid random-intercept model is \[
y_{ij}^{(m)} = \beta_1 + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_k^{W}\bigl(x_{k,ij} - \bar x_{k\cdot j}\bigr)
            + \sum_{k=1}^{K} \beta_k^{B}\,\bar x_{k\cdot j} + \zeta_j + \epsilon_{ij}^{(m)},
\qquad \zeta_j \sim N(0,\sigma^2_{\text{school}}),\ \ \epsilon_{ij}^{(m)} \sim N(0,\sigma^2).
\] The intercept \(\beta_1\), the within-school slopes \(\beta^W\), and the between-school slopes \(\beta^B\) are collected into the fixed-effect block \(\beta_{\text{FE}} = (\beta_1, \beta^W, \beta^B)^\top \in
\mathbb{R}^{p}\), \(p = 2K+1\) — the sole inferential target of this chapter. Marginalizing over \(\zeta_j\) gives a Gaussian with block-diagonal covariance \[
V = \operatorname{blkdiag}_j\!\bigl(\sigma^2 I_{n_j} + \sigma^2_{\text{school}}\,\mathbf 1_{n_j}\mathbf 1_{n_j}^\top\bigr),
\qquad \Omega = V^{-1}.
\] Writing \(X\) for the \(n\times p\) hybrid design (its columns are the constant, the within-school deviations \((x_{k,ij}-\bar x_{k\cdot j})\), and the school means \(\bar x_{k\cdot j}\)) and stacking students into \(y^{(m)}\in\mathbb R^n\), the per-PV GLS log-likelihood at fixed \(V\) is \[
\log L_m^0(\beta_{\text{FE}};V) = -\tfrac12\bigl(y^{(m)}-X\beta_{\text{FE}}\bigr)^\top\Omega\bigl(y^{(m)}-X\beta_{\text{FE}}\bigr) + \text{const}(V).
\] The constant absorbs \(-\tfrac12\log\det V\), which does not depend on \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) at fixed \(V\) — the same \(\log\det V\) term that, because it is independent of \(y\), will make the variance-component score nonlinear later in this chapter.
A word on survey weights. PISA estimation is survey-weighted, and I have written \(\Omega\) without a weight matrix only for readability. Two facts make this harmless for the present identity. First, the survey weights index students and the sampling design — not which plausible value — so they are common across the \(M\) PVs and enter every \(L_m^0\) identically; that PV-invariance is the only thing the proof below uses. Second, our pipeline does not inject design-based uncertainty through the likelihood at all: \(L_m^0\) carries the model, and the design-based variance enters separately, through the external BRR-Fay target of Section 5.1. So whether one folds the (PV-common) weights into \(\Omega\) as a weighted pseudo-likelihood or keeps \(L_m^0\) as the unweighted working likelihood, the centering identity of this chapter is unaffected. The weights matter enormously for the variance — they are the heart of Section 5.1 — but not for the center.
crumbs: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
text:
3.3 The stacked fractional bridge
All three pipelines that use one MCMC sample from the stacked fractional bridge \[
\log q_{\text{SWL}}(\psi) = \log p(\psi) + \frac{1}{M}\sum_{m=1}^{M}\log L_m^0(\psi) + C,
\qquad \psi = (\beta_{\text{FE}},\sigma^2_{\text{school}},\sigma^2).
\] The \(1/M\) exponent is the crucial design choice, and it is worth seeing why in information terms. Each \(\log L_m^0\) carries Fisher information \(X^\top\Omega X\) for \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\). The naive stack \(\sum_m \log L_m^0\) would accumulate \(M\cdot X^\top\Omega X\) — fabricating \(M\)-fold replication, as if the ten plausible values were ten independent surveys rather than ten draws from one latent distribution. Multiplying each contribution by \(1/M\) restores exactly one dataset’s worth of information: \(q_{\text{SWL}} \propto p(\psi)\,[\prod_m L_m^0(\psi)]^{1/M}\) is the geometric mean of the \(M\) per-PV likelihoods. One MCMC fit, single-PV information content. In code the exponent is the per-row weight \(1/M\) on a long-stacked dataset (stack_fractional_posterior(); see Section 8.1).
crumbs: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
text:
3.4 Regularity conditions
The proof uses four conditions; I separate the one that controls exactness from the rest.

R0 — flat prior. \(p(\beta_{\text{FE}})\propto 1\). The identity is exact in the MLE/mode regime. A proper Gaussian prior does not break the stacked-versus-per-PV correspondence — under a common prior the stacked-bridge MAP still coincides with the per-PV MAP (verified in falsify_theorem22.R §F3). What a proper prior adds is shrinkage relative to the flat-prior Rubin target; where a weakly informative prior is used for sampler stability we keep it diffuse enough that this shrinkage gap is negligible.
R2 — common design. \(X\) (and any PV-common survey weighting) is identical across the \(M\) PVs. Automatic for PISA: plausible values vary the outcome, not the covariates — provided the cluster means \(\bar x_{k\cdot j}\) are computed once from the full sample, not per imputation.
R3a — common plug-in \(V\). A single pilot \((\hat\sigma^2,\hat\sigma^2_{\text{school}})\) is fixed, so \(\Omega\) is the same matrix in every per-PV solve and in the stacked solve. This is the condition that makes the identity exact; its relation to the real (joint) MCMC and to conventional separate-fit pooling is the subject of the honesty section below.
R5 — invertibility. \(X^\top\Omega X\) is positive definite — equivalently, \(X\) has full column rank and \(\Omega\succ 0\) — which holds for the PISA USA and KOR designs.
crumbs: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
text:
3.5 Theorem 2.2 (the licensing identity)
Let \(\hat\beta_{\text{FE},m} = (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega\,y^{(m)}\) be the per-PV GLS estimate at the common plug-in \(\Omega\) of R3a, and let \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}} = M^{-1}\sum_m
\hat\beta_{\text{FE},m}\) be the corresponding (fixed-\(\Omega\)) Rubin pooled estimate. Let \(\hat\beta_{\text{FE}}^q\) be the maximizer of \(\log q_{\text{SWL}}\) over \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) at that same fixed \(V\).

Theorem 2.2. Under R0, R2, R3a, R5, \[
\hat\beta_{\text{FE}}^q
= \arg\max_{\beta_{\text{FE}}}\frac1M\sum_{m=1}^M\log L_m^0(\beta_{\text{FE}};V)
= \frac1M\sum_{m=1}^M \hat\beta_{\text{FE},m}
= \bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}.
\] Moreover, under R0 the \(q\)-marginal in \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) is Gaussian, so its mode and mean coincide: \(\hat\beta_{\text{FE}}^q = \mathbb E_q[\beta_{\text{FE}}]\). The equality is exact in arithmetic; in finite precision the residual sits at the floating-point level (verified numerically in Part IV).

Two clarifications belong with the statement. First, the “mode = mean” clause is what lets Pipeline C read its point estimate straight off the stacked posterior mean — no separate per-PV refit is needed to obtain the center. Second, and this is essential to state honestly: \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) here is the fixed-\(\Omega\) pooled estimate. It equals the number a practitioner would get from \(M\) separately fitted mixed models — each of which re-estimates its own \(\Omega_m = V_m^{-1}\) — only up to the R3a-vs-R3b gap of the next section. The separate-fit pool is an R3b object; Theorem 2.2 is an R3a statement.

3.5.1 Proof
Step 1 — per-PV score. Differentiating \(\log L_m^0\) in \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\), \[
\nabla_{\beta_{\text{FE}}}\log L_m^0 = X^\top\Omega\,y^{(m)} - X^\top\Omega X\,\beta_{\text{FE}}.
\] This score is affine-linear in \(y^{(m)}\), and the term \(X^\top\Omega X\,\beta_{\text{FE}}\) does not depend on \(m\). That linearity is the load-bearing fact of the whole theorem.
Step 2 — stacked gradient. Under R0 the prior gradient vanishes, so \[
\nabla_{\beta_{\text{FE}}}\Bigl[\tfrac1M\textstyle\sum_m\log L_m^0\Bigr]
= \frac1M\sum_{m=1}^M\bigl[X^\top\Omega\,y^{(m)} - X^\top\Omega X\,\beta_{\text{FE}}\bigr]
= X^\top\Omega\,\bar y - X^\top\Omega X\,\beta_{\text{FE}},
\] with \(\bar y = M^{-1}\sum_m y^{(m)}\). R2 (common \(X\), common weighting) and R3a (common \(\Omega\)) are exactly what let \(X^\top\Omega X\) and \(X^\top\Omega\) come out of the sum; if either varied with \(m\), the average could not collapse to a single matrix acting on \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\).
Step 3 — stacked normal equation. Setting the gradient to zero and using R5, \[
X^\top\Omega X\,\hat\beta_{\text{FE}}^q = X^\top\Omega\,\bar y
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\hat\beta_{\text{FE}}^q = (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega\,\bar y.
\]
Step 4 — average the per-PV equations. Each per-PV normal equation is \(X^\top\Omega X\,\hat\beta_{\text{FE},m} = X^\top\Omega\,y^{(m)}\) (same \(\Omega\), by R3a); averaging over \(m\), \[
X^\top\Omega X\,\Bigl[\tfrac1M\textstyle\sum_m\hat\beta_{\text{FE},m}\Bigr] = X^\top\Omega\,\bar y.
\] The stacked equation (Step 3) and the averaged per-PV equations have identical right-hand sides; by R5 the solution is unique, so \(\hat\beta_{\text{FE}}^q = M^{-1}\sum_m\hat\beta_{\text{FE},m} =
\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\).
Step 5 — mode equals mean. At fixed \(V\) (R3a) with a flat prior (R0), \(\log q_{\text{SWL}}\) is quadratic in \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) with Hessian \(-X^\top\Omega X \prec 0\) (R5), so the block is exactly Gaussian and its mode equals its mean. Note that under R3a the variance components are fixed, not sampled, so for \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) the marginal and conditional posteriors coincide — the Gaussianity is genuine, not an artifact of integrating out \(\sigma^2\) (which would generally leave heavier, \(t\)-like tails, exactly the phenomenon behind the variance-component discussion below). \(\qquad\square\)
In one sentence: “average the gradients” equals “evaluate at \(\bar y\),” so the stacked bridge solves the same normal equations as GLS on the PV-averaged outcome.
crumbs: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
section: 3.6 R3a vs R3b: exactness and its honest limit
text:
3.6 R3a vs R3b: exactness and its honest limit
This is the place I most want to be careful with you, because glossing it would undermine the whole pipeline. The proof lives in R3a world: one pilot \(\hat V\) frozen and reused in every solve. There the identity is exact, and our Part IV checks put the residual at the floating-point level.
The deployed Pipeline C MCMC lives in R3b world: it samples \((\beta_{\text{FE}},\sigma^2_{\text{school}},\sigma^2)\) jointly, so \(\Omega\) is not pinned but co-evolves with the chain, and the marginal \(p(\beta_{\text{FE}}\mid\text{data})\) is a mixture over \(V\). Because \(\partial\hat\beta(V)/\partial V\neq 0\), the R3a algebra does not transfer at theorem level to the R3b mode. The same point explains B1’s subtlety: the conventional practice — ten separately fitted mixed models, each with its own \(\hat V_m\), then pooled — is likewise an R3b comparison, and differs from the fixed-\(\Omega\) \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) of Theorem 2.2 by a term of the same kind.
The point is not that this gap is harmless but that we measure it rather than assume it away. For each coefficient we report the standardized discrepancy \[
\Delta_{\text{R3b},k} = \frac{\bigl|\hat\beta_k^{\text{R3b}} - \hat\beta_k^{\text{R3a}}\bigr|}{\sqrt{T_{\text{MI},kk}}},
\] where \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is the total MI variance defined in Section 5.1 — borrowed here only as a scale, so the ratio asks “is the R3a-vs-R3b shift small relative to the uncertainty we ultimately report?” We apply a traffic-light rule — green \(<0.01\), yellow \(\le 0.02\), red \(>0.02\). There is reason to expect green at PISA scale: the variance-component posterior concentrates tightly (the posterior SD of \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\) is a small fraction of its mean), so \(\hat V\) is a good stand-in for the posterior-mean \(V\). But that is an empirical expectation, checked in the known-truth simulation of Part IV and on the real data in Part V — not a theorem. If a coefficient lands red, we report the R3b result directly rather than lean on the identity.
crumbs: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
section: 3.7 Why the identity does not extend to the variance components
text:
3.7 Why the identity does not extend to the variance components
Theorem 2.2 exploits one structure only: the FE score is linear in \(y^{(m)}\). The score for \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\) is not. Differentiating the marginal log-likelihood gives \[
\frac{\partial \log L_m^0}{\partial \sigma^2_{\text{school}}}
= -\tfrac12\operatorname{tr}\!\Bigl(\Omega\,\tfrac{\partial V}{\partial \sigma^2_{\text{school}}}\Bigr)
+ \tfrac12\,r_m^\top\,\Omega\,\tfrac{\partial V}{\partial \sigma^2_{\text{school}}}\,\Omega\,r_m,
\qquad r_m = y^{(m)}-X\beta_{\text{FE}}.
\] The first term does not involve \(y^{(m)}\) at all (it is the derivative of the \(\log\det V\) we set aside earlier, and it cannot be averaged away); the second is quadratic in \(y^{(m)}\), and \(M^{-1}\sum_m y^{(m)}(y^{(m)})^\top \neq \bar y\,\bar y^\top\) unless all PVs are identical. So “average the \(m\) scores” \(\neq\) “evaluate at \(\bar y\),” and the stacked mode for \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\) does not equal the Rubin average of the per-PV variance-component estimates. We expect this drift to be small at PISA scale, but the chapter does not rely on any particular figure — we report the measured value in Part IV. What matters here is the structural fact that the drift is nonzero, unlike the \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) case. This is the formal reason the calibration in Section 6.1 touches the \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) block only, and why calibrating the variance components is deferred to the follow-up paper.
crumbs: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
section: 3.8 Pipeline B does not inherit Theorem 2.2
text:
3.8 Pipeline B does not inherit Theorem 2.2
It is worth saying explicitly that Pipeline B’s point estimates do not enjoy the exact identity. Pipeline B forms per-PV estimates as Pareto-smoothed importance-weighted averages of the shared bridge draws, \(\hat\beta_{\text{FE},m}^{\text{PSIS}} = \sum_s \tilde w_{m,s}\,\beta_s\) (Section 7.1). The algebraic cancellation in the proof requires the exact maximizer of the gradient equation; a finite-sample weighted average is not that maximizer. PSIS recovers the per-PV target only as the number of draws \(S\to\infty\); in the finite-variance regime (\(\hat k_m < 0.5\)) the residual shrinks at the \(O_p(S^{-1/2})\) rate, and more slowly as the Pareto shape \(\hat k_m\) rises toward the unreliable regime \(\hat k_m \ge 0.7\), at which point Pipeline B falls back to A or C-Direct (Vehtari et al. 2024). Theorem 2.2’s exactness is a Pipeline C-Direct statement, full stop.
crumbs: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate

title: 3  The Licensing Identity: One Fit Gives the Pooled Point Estimate
text:
3.9 Lineage and verification
In the spirit of Si and Reiter (2011), who compared posterior simulation against the combining rules for multiple imputation, the single-level version of this identity is classical; Theorem 2.2 extends it to the two-level Allison-hybrid GLS setting, to fractional PV stacking, and adds the explicit R3a/R3b decomposition that does not arise in the single-level case. We do not ask you to take the identity on faith: the known-truth simulation in Part IV reports an exactness grid — coefficients across a factorial of \(J\), cluster size, ICC, and \(M\) — and we expect every cell to pass to floating-point precision under R3a, shown beside the contrasting \(\Delta_{\text{R3b}}\) sensitivities.
What this chapter licensed: the center. One stacked fit returns the fixed-\(\Omega\) \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) as its posterior mean — exactly under R3a, with a measured gap under R3b and relative to conventional separate-fit pooling. The variance story — what \(T_{\text{MI}} = \bar U +
(1+1/M)B\) is, and why the between-imputation term lives in parameter space — is where we turn next.




Si, Yajuan, and Jerome P. Reiter. 2011. “A Comparison of Posterior Simulation and Inference by Combining Rules for Multiple Imputation.” Journal of Statistical Theory and Practice 5 (2): 335–47. https://doi.org/10.1080/15598608.2011.10412032.


Vehtari, Aki, Daniel Simpson, Andrew Gelman, Yuling Yao, and Jonah Gabry. 2024. “Pareto Smoothed Importance Sampling.” Journal of Machine Learning Research 25 (72): 1–58. https://jmlr.org/papers/v25/19-556.html.

title: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance
text:
4.1 The missing arrow
Your comment C3 sits right next to the term \((1+1/M)B\) in the Rubin total variance, and it asks the exactly right question: isn’t that \(B\) a variance of the plausible values \(y\), not of the model parameters \(\beta\)? How do the two connect? Your email broadened it to both pieces — “my best guess is that \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is coming from the plausible values \(y\) for the between and within.”
The short answer: no — both pieces live entirely in parameter space. The longer answer, which is this chapter, is that your instinct is half right and worth taking seriously. The variability in \(T_{\text{MI}}\) does originate in the \(M\) different plausible-value vectors \(y^{(1)},\dots,y^{(M)}\). But it is measured in the space of the regression coefficients \(\hat\beta_m \in \mathbb R^{p}\) (with \(p = \dim\beta_{\text{FE}} = 2K+1\), as in Section 3.1). The arrow from one space to the other is the GLS map, and drawing it explicitly is the whole job here.
crumbs: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance

title: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance
section: 4.2 The GLS map carries variance from \(y\)-space to \(\beta\)-space
text:
4.2 The GLS map carries variance from \(y\)-space to \(\beta\)-space
From Section 3.1, the per-PV GLS estimator at a fixed precision \(\Omega = V^{-1}\) is \[
\hat\beta_m = (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega\,y^{(m)} =: F\,y^{(m)}, \qquad m = 1,\dots,M,
\] where \(F = (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega\) is a fixed \(p\times n\) matrix (the same \(\Omega\) for every PV, under R3a). The single structural fact that matters is that \(F\) is linear. Linearity has an immediate covariance consequence: if we ask how the scatter across imputations propagates from \(y\)-space into \(\beta\)-space, the sandwich identity gives the exact answer, \[
\operatorname{Cov}_m\!\bigl[\hat\beta_m\bigr]
= F\,\operatorname{Cov}_m\!\bigl[y^{(m)}\bigr]\,F^\top
= (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega
  \underbrace{\operatorname{Cov}_m\!\bigl[y^{(m)}\bigr]}_{n\times n,\ \text{in } y\text{-space}}
  \Omega X (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}.
\] This sandwich is the missing arrow. The plausible-value covariance — an \(n\times n\) object in observation space, the IRT measurement uncertainty — is pressed between \(F\) on the left and \(F^\top\) on the right and carried into a \(p\times p\) object in parameter space. From this point on, everything Rubin’s rules do is done in \(\mathbb R^{p\times p}\). (Here \(\operatorname{Cov}_m[\cdot]\) is the population imputation-posterior covariance; the \(B\) defined below is its finite-sample plug-in from \(M\) proper, exchangeable draws — which is exactly why the \(1/(M-1)\) and the \((1+1/M)\) correction appear.)
crumbs: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance

title: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance
section: 4.3 Both Rubin pieces are parameter-space matrices
text:
4.3 Both Rubin pieces are parameter-space matrices
With the map in hand, the two combining-rule pieces are unambiguous \(p\times p\) matrices.
Within-imputation variance \(\bar U\). For each PV, the BRR-Fay sandwich (PISA Technical Report Formula 10.9; \(a_d = 1/[R(1-k)^2] = 0.05\) for \(R=80,\,k=0.5\)) is \[
\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}} = a_d\sum_{r=1}^R \bigl(\hat\beta_m^{(r)}-\hat\beta_m\bigr)\bigl(\hat\beta_m^{(r)}-\hat\beta_m\bigr)^\top,
\qquad \hat\beta_m^{(r)} = (X^\top\Omega^{(r)}X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega^{(r)}y^{(m)},
\] already a \(p\times p\) object built from deviations of coefficient vectors. Its PV-average is \(\bar U = M^{-1}\sum_m \hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\) — the design-based sampling uncertainty in \(\hat\beta_m\) that would remain even if the latent scores were known perfectly.
Between-imputation variance \(B\). With \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}} = M^{-1}\sum_m\hat\beta_m\), \[
B = \frac{1}{M-1}\sum_{m=1}^M \bigl(\hat\beta_m-\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\bigr)\bigl(\hat\beta_m-\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\bigr)^\top.
\] Look at what enters this formula: only the \(M\) coefficient vectors \(\hat\beta_1,\dots,\hat\beta_M\). The outcome vectors \(y^{(m)}\) do not appear. \(B\) is the sample covariance of \(M\) points in \(\mathbb R^{p}\) — it measures how far the GLS solution moves as the IRT model draws a fresh realization of the latent outcome. This is the direct answer to C3: \((1+1/M)B\) is not the variance of the PVs; it is the (corrected) covariance of the \(M\) GLS coefficient estimates, each the image of one PV vector under \(F\).
Total variance and the \((1+1/M)\) correction. \[
T_{\text{MI}} = \bar U + \Bigl(1+\tfrac1M\Bigr)B.
\] The factor decomposes as \((1+1/M)B = B + B/M\): the first term is the between-imputation variance (already unbiased, thanks to the \(1/(M-1)\) in \(B\)); the extra \(B/M\) is the simulation error in the finite-\(M\) point estimate \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\), which averages only \(M\) imputed estimates around the unobservable infinite-\(M\) value (Rubin 1987). It is not a bias in \(B\). At \(M=10\) the inflation is \(1.1\) (and \(1.2\) at the \(M=5\) used below); as \(M\to\infty\) it vanishes and \(T_{\text{MI}}\to\bar U+B\). Every term is \(p\times p\); the whole apparatus operates in parameter space from start to finish.
crumbs: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance

title: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance
section: 4.4 “Between and within” — the symmetry your email asked about
text:
4.4 “Between and within” — the symmetry your email asked about
Your email asked specifically whether the connection holds “for the between and within.” It does, and the symmetry is worth stating because it is the cleanest way to see that nothing is left in \(y\)-space. Both \(\bar U\) and \(B\) are \(\beta\)-space covariances produced by the same GLS machinery — they differ in what is perturbed, and in whether the perturbation lands on the map or on its input:

\(\bar U\) holds the PV draw fixed and perturbs the sampling design (the \(R=80\) replicate weights, which reweight \(\Omega \to \Omega^{(r)}\)); the resulting scatter of \(\hat\beta_m^{(r)}\) is a \(\beta\)-space covariance, then averaged over PVs. Here the perturbation falls on the map itself, \(F\to F^{(r)} = (X^\top\Omega^{(r)}X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega^{(r)}\).
\(B\) holds the sampling design fixed and perturbs the IRT draw (a new \(y^{(m)}\)); the resulting scatter of \(\hat\beta_m\) is a \(\beta\)-space covariance. Here the map \(F\) is held fixed and only its input \(y^{(m)}\) varies.

Two different sources of uncertainty — replicate weights for \(\bar U\), plausible-value draws for \(B\) — both measured in \(\hat\beta\)-space by the same GLS machinery. There is no residual sense in which any component of \(T_{\text{MI}}\) “is” a variance of \(y\).
crumbs: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance

title: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance
text:
4.5 Fraction of missing information
For a single coefficient, the fraction of missing information is \[
\gamma_k = \frac{(1+1/M)\,B_{kk}}{T_{\text{MI},kk}},
\] the share of total uncertainty in \(\hat\beta_k\) owed to not observing the latent scores directly. Illustrative magnitudes (typical of PISA secondary analyses, not measured here): within-school slopes \(\beta^W\) have low FMI, roughly \(0.03\)–\(0.08\), because student-level measurement noise averages down over thousands of students; between-school slopes \(\beta^B\) are higher, roughly \(0.10\)–\(0.20\), because the contextual effect is identified from only \(J\sim 150\)–\(500\) schools, so a fresh PV draw reshuffles school means more consequentially. The actual values are reported in the simulation diagnostics (Part IV) and the PISA runs (Part V).

title: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance
text:
4.6 Worked arithmetic (illustrative numbers throughout)
To make the propagation concrete, take a synthetic setting — \(J=10\) schools, \(n=200\) students, \(M=5\) PVs, \(\text{ICC}\approx0.30\) — and one coefficient, the within-school ESCS slope, in PISA-scale units\(^2\). These numbers are illustrative, drawn from a synthetic micro-example, not from a pipeline run.

From these, \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}} = 40.00\), \(\bar U = 2.260\), and \[
B = \tfrac14\sum_m(\hat\beta_m-40.00)^2 = \tfrac{0.2570}{4} = 0.0643,
\qquad (1+\tfrac15)B = 1.2\times0.0643 = 0.0771,
\qquad T_{\text{MI}} = 2.260+0.0771 = 2.3371.
\] In standard-error terms, \(\sqrt{\bar U} = 1.503\) versus \(\sqrt{T_{\text{MI}}} = 1.529\) — the SE rises about \(1.7\%\). Small, but always in the same direction: ignoring \(B\) (using only \(\bar U\), or the stacked-bridge replicate variance \(V_{q,\text{rep}}\approx\bar U\) of Section 5.1) makes the interval too narrow and under-covers, because it pretends a different IRT draw would have left \(\hat\beta\) exactly where it is.
For a between-school coefficient \(\beta^B\) in the same synthetic setting, the school-level design inflates \(B\) more: illustratively \(\bar U^{(B)} = 18.0\), \(B^{(B)} = 2.25\), \((1+1/M)B^{(B)} = 2.70\), \(T_{\text{MI}}^{(B)} = 20.7\), so \(\sqrt{\bar U^{(B)}} = 4.243\) versus \(\sqrt{T_{\text{MI}}^{(B)}} =
4.550\) — about a \(7\%\) gap, i.e. FMI \(\approx 0.13\). A nominal 95% interval that drops \(B\) here covers about \(93\%\): coverage erodes more slowly than the SE near the center of a Gaussian, so a \(7\%\) SE shortfall costs roughly two coverage points, not seven — but the loss is real and one-directional.
crumbs: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance

title: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance
text:
4.7 Forward links
The between-imputation piece is now fully placed: \(B\) is the sample covariance of the \(M\) parameter vectors \(\hat\beta_m\), tied to the plausible values through the linear GLS map. The next chapter (Section 5.1) assembles the full \(T_{\text{MI}}\) from the \(R=80\) replicate weights — the \(M(R{+}1)\) fast lme4 REML refits that build each \(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\), and the rubin_pool_matrix() routine that combines the pieces.
And the payoff for Pipeline C is now visible. Because both \(\bar U\) and \(B\) are honest covariance matrices in parameter space — not residuals, not score dispersions, not approximations — their sum \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is a well-defined, Cholesky-factorizable covariance target in the same space and units as the raw posterior covariance of the stacked MCMC draws. That dimensional match is exactly what lets the Cholesky Calibration Correction reshape the posterior to \(T_{\text{MI}}\) in one shot (Section 6.1). Had \(T_{\text{MI}}\) really been a variance of \(y\), the dimensions would not have matched and the calibration would be ill-defined. Tracing the GLS map from end to end is what guarantees they do.




Rubin, Donald B. 1987. Multiple Imputation for Nonresponse in Surveys. Wiley.
crumbs: 4  From Plausible-Value Variance to Parameter Variance

title: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly
text:
5.1 Your question, squarely answered
This is the chapter you asked for most directly. Two of your comments land on the same object:
Both instincts are exactly right, and I want to say so plainly: \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is model-specific, and it does require fitting many models. The only correction is which fits. They are \(M(R{+}1) = 810\) fast frequentist lme4 REML fits — not MCMC chains — assembled by a self-contained subroutine, BRRFay-2L, that uses only lme4 and the published replicate weights and never calls any pipeline’s sampler. So “without A or B” does not mean the target is free or pipeline-specific; it means the variance machinery is orthogonal to the MCMC.
crumbs: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly

title: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly
text:
C4 (on the external \(T_{\text{MI}}\)): I don’t understand where this comes from? isn’t it model specific and needs to be computed by estimating multiple models?
C2 (on \(T_{\text{MI}}\) generally): How do we get this without using A or B methods?
crumbs: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly

title: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly
text:
5.2 The per-PV BRR-Fay sandwich
PISA 2022 ships a final student weight W_FSTUWT and \(R = 80\) replicate weights W_FSTURWT1…W_FSTURWT80, built by Fay’s balanced repeated replication with shrinkage \(k = 0.5\). The PISA Technical Report (Formula 10.9) anchors the design variance with the multiplier \[
a_d = \frac{1}{R(1-k)^2} = \frac{1}{80\times 0.25} = 0.05.
\] For each plausible value, let \(\hat\beta_m\) be the full-weight fixed-effect estimate and \(\hat\beta_m^{(r)}\) the estimate under replicate weight \(r\). Conceptually these are the GLS solves \(\hat\beta_m = (X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega y^{(m)}\) and \(\hat\beta_m^{(r)} = (X^\top\Omega^{(r)}X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega^{(r)}y^{(m)}\) (from Section 4.1); in the implementation each is obtained by a survey-weighted two-level REML fit, lme4::lmer(y ~ … + (1|school), weights = w_r), with the replicate weights scaled per Pfeffermann’s two-level convention. The per-PV BRR-Fay sandwich is the matrix form of the Fay variance, \[
\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}} = a_d\sum_{r=1}^R \bigl(\hat\beta_m^{(r)}-\hat\beta_m\bigr)\bigl(\hat\beta_m^{(r)}-\hat\beta_m\bigr)^\top \in \mathbb R^{p\times p},
\] and the Rubin assembly of Section 4.1 gives \(\bar U = M^{-1}\sum_m\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\), the between term \(B\), and \[
T_{\text{MI}} = \bar U + \Bigl(1+\tfrac1M\Bigr)B.
\] This is the whole of \(T_{\text{MI}}\): a \(p\times p\) object in parameter space, model-specific (it encodes the two-level model), assembled from many model fits — exactly as you suspected.
crumbs: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly

title: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly
section: 5.3 The 810-fit count, and why the fits are cheap
text:
5.3 The 810-fit count, and why the fits are cheap
Per PV: one full-weight fit plus \(R = 80\) replicate fits \(= 81\). Over \(M = 10\) PVs, \[
M(R+1) = 10 \times 81 = 810 \text{ mini-fits per country}.
\] That is the literal “multiple models” of your C4. The decisive fact is the cost of each. These are lme4 REML fits — iterative, but fast: in the deployed code (brr_fay_U_one_pv(), Section 8.1) each replicate is a full survey-weighted lmer(..., REML = TRUE) that re-estimates its own variance components, which is the standard (and conservative) BRR practice. I benchmarked one such fit at PISA-USA scale — synthetic \(n = 4{,}620\), \(J = 154\), two slopes — and it returns in about 0.012 s (code/01_brrfay_timing.R). At that rate the full 810-fit loop is on the order of ten seconds single-threaded; with a richer covariate set or default lmer controls, allow up to ~1–2 s per fit and the loop is still only 15–30 minutes. Either way it is orders of magnitude below MCMC.
The contrast is the point (the single-MCMC figures below are the times we measured on the real PISA data in Section 13.1; the Pipeline A total is ten times the measured single fit):










Pipeline A
BRR-Fay loop
Pipeline C MCMC




Fits per country
\(M=10\) MCMC
\(810\) lme4 REML
\(1\) MCMC


Cost per fit
$\(10–26 min | **\)$0.01–2 s (measured/bounded)**
$\(10–26 min |
| **Total per country** | **\)\(2–4 h** | **seconds to ~30 min** | **\)$10–26 min**




(The single-MCMC range is the measured USA-to-Korea span, ~10 min and ~26 min respectively, Section 13.1.) Had one tried to compute BRR-Fay variance by MCMC refits, the cost would be \(810\times 10\text{–}26\) min — weeks of wall-clock per country. That is why BRRFay-2L is committed to frequentist REML: not a corner cut, but the only feasible route, and a very cheap one. The PV loop parallelizes trivially over \(M\) cores, so even the pessimistic bound drops to a few minutes.
An optional speed-up, not the default. If the variance components are pinned at a single pilot \(\hat V\) (the R3a regime of Section 3.1), each replicate collapses to a genuine closed-form GLS solve — faster still, and exactly the setting in which Theorem 2.2 holds. The deployed code does not pin them; it re-fits fully per replicate. We flag the pinned variant only because it is the bridge between this chapter’s cost argument and Chapter 3’s exactness argument.
crumbs: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly

title: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly
text:
5.4 The BRRFay-2L algorithm
Deterministic throughout — an outer PV loop, an inner replicate loop, then a standard Rubin assembly. No sampler, no convergence audit beyond each REML fit’s own.
Algorithm BRRFay-2L
Inputs:  long-form data (M PVs, R=80 replicate weights, design X);
         a_d = 1/(R(1-k)^2) = 0.05
  1.  for m = 1..M:
        β̂_m ← weighted-REML fixef of y^(m) at full weight        # lme4::lmer
        for r = 1..R:
          β̂_m^(r) ← weighted-REML fixef of y^(m) at replicate weight W^(r)
        Û_m ← a_d · Σ_r (β̂_m^(r) − β̂_m)(β̂_m^(r) − β̂_m)ᵀ
  2.  β̄_Rubin ← M^{-1} Σ_m β̂_m
  3.  Ū ← M^{-1} Σ_m Û_m
  4.  B ← (M−1)^{-1} Σ_m (β̂_m − β̄_Rubin)(β̂_m − β̄_Rubin)ᵀ
  5.  T_MI ← Ū + (1 + 1/M) B
Output:  {β̂_m}, {Û_m}, β̄_Rubin, Ū, B, T_MI
        (optional R3a speed-up: pin variance components at a pilot V̂ → closed-form GLS solves)

text:
5.5 BRRFay-2L is shared by all three pipelines
Here is the direct answer to C2’s “without A or B.” BRRFay-2L is not part of Pipeline A, B, or C-Direct — it is a pipeline-independent module that runs once and feeds all three:









Pipeline
CCC center
CCC target
BRRFay-2L output used




A (orthodox)
\(\hat\beta_m\) per PV
\(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\) per PV
per-PV sandwiches (\(M\) calls)


B (PSIS)
\(\hat\beta_m^{\text{PSIS}}\) per PV
\(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\) per PV
per-PV sandwiches (\(M\) calls)


C-Direct
\(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\)
\(T_{\text{MI}}\) (pooled)
pooled \(T_{\text{MI}}\) directly



Its inputs are only the replicate weights, the design, the PV vectors — its output is pure lme4 plus matrix algebra, independent of whether inference uses Stan, brms, or no sampler at all. No pipeline avoids the 810 fits; none pays them twice. Pipeline C’s economy over Pipeline A is not a saving on the variance target — both assemble the identical \(T_{\text{MI}}\) — but purely a saving on MCMC count, ten chains down to one. (One small asymmetry worth naming: Pipeline A uses its ten per-PV MCMC posterior means as CCC centers and the BRR-Fay sandwiches as targets. Pipeline C-Direct, by contrast, centers its single calibrated cloud at the external \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — the BRR-Fay GLS pooled estimate itself, passed in as a numeric vector, not the raw stacked posterior mean — so its reported point equals Pipeline A’s exactly, and the R3a→R3b gap of Section 3.1 becomes a verification diagnostic that never enters the deliverable (Section 10.1, Section 13.1). The shared 810-fit cost is unaffected.) How each pipeline binds its target to the operator is Section 6.1.

title: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly
section: 5.6 Why the single MCMC’s own covariance cannot substitute
text:
5.6 Why the single MCMC’s own covariance cannot substitute
A tempting shortcut: surely the one stacked MCMC already carries the variance, making the external loop redundant? It does not, and the reason is structural rather than numerical. Define the stacked-bridge replicate variance \[
V_{q,\text{rep}} = a_d\sum_{r=1}^R \bigl(\hat\beta^{q,(r)}-\hat\beta^q\bigr)\bigl(\hat\beta^{q,(r)}-\hat\beta^q\bigr)^\top,
\] where \(\hat\beta^{q,(r)}\) is the stacked-bridge point estimate under replicate \(r\). Under R3a and Theorem 2.2 applied per replicate (Section 3.1), \(\hat\beta^{q,(r)} = (X^\top\Omega^{(r)}X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega^{(r)}\bar y\) — a GLS solve on the PV-average \(\bar y\). The between-PV scatter is therefore collapsed into \(\bar y\) before any replicate perturbation, and is lost: \[
V_{q,\text{rep}} \;\overset{\text{R3a}}{=}\; \bar U \;\preceq\; T_{\text{MI}} = \bar U + (1+\tfrac1M)B.
\] Read this in two registers. The Loewner inequality \(\preceq\) is the R3a statement: at a common plug-in \(V\), \(V_{q,\text{rep}}\) equals \(\bar U\) exactly and \((1+1/M)B \succeq 0\). The regime-free fact — the one that holds under R3a or R3b — is structural rather than Loewner: because \(\bar y\) collapses the between-PV scatter before any replicate perturbation, \(V_{q,\text{rep}}\) misses the entire \((1+1/M)B\) term outright. Under the deployed R3b sampler \(V_{q,\text{rep}} \approx \bar U\) is an empirical expectation (consistent with how Section 3.1 treats R3b), checked rather than assumed. So calibrating to \(V_{q,\text{rep}}\) rather than \(T_{\text{MI}}\) understates the SE by about \(\gamma/2\) — a first-order approximation, tight for the small within-school \(\gamma\) but running a little under the exact value for the larger between-school \(\gamma\) (the \(7\%\) quoted below is exact, against a \(\gamma/2\approx6.5\%\) linear estimate) — and under-covers, with \(\gamma = (1+1/M)B/T_{\text{MI}}\) the fraction of missing information. In the Section 4.1 micro-example, the within-school slope (\(\gamma\approx0.03\)) loses about \(1.7\%\) of SE — a nominal 95% interval still covers about 94% — while the between-school slope (\(\gamma\approx0.13\)) loses about \(7\%\), dropping coverage to roughly 93%. Modest per coefficient, but systematic, one-directional, and worse for the contextual (\(\beta^B\)) effects that are often the substantive target.
Two diagnostic ratios audit any implementation: \[
\rho_1 = \frac{V_{q,\text{rep}}}{\bar U}\quad(\text{expected}\approx 1,\ \textbf{measured not assumed}),
\qquad
\rho_2 = \frac{T_{\text{MI}}}{\bar U} = 1+\gamma \quad(\text{the FMI the single fit cannot see}).
\] That \(\rho_2 > 1\) — and that it cannot be recovered from the stacked fit no matter how long the chain — is the numerical statement of why an external \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is genuinely necessary. As a guardrail, pipeline_c_direct.R refuses any Pipeline C call whose \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\) is a self-target (equal to the raw posterior covariance) or an obvious mock matrix (Section 8.1) — a deliberate block against exactly the shortcut that would quietly under-cover.
crumbs: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly

title: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly
section: 5.7 The honest one-liner, and what comes next
text:
5.7 The honest one-liner, and what comes next
\(T_{\text{MI}}\) is model-specific and takes 810 model fits — but cheap lme4 REML fits, a shared subroutine of seconds to (pessimistically) a few minutes per country, independent of any pipeline’s MCMC. Pipeline C-Direct’s sole advantage over Pipeline A is the MCMC count, one versus ten; the variance target costs the same either way, and neither pipeline can read it off a single posterior.
The next chapter (Section 6.1) shows how \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) and \(T_{\text{MI}}\) are injected into the Cholesky Calibration Correction as center and target, and why the operator acts on \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) alone. The implementation is assemble_pisa_brr_fay_target() with its helper brr_fay_U_one_pv() (Section 8.1).
crumbs: 5  Where the External Variance Comes From: BRR-Fay Assembly

title: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas
text:
6.1 Two questions in one comment
Your fifth comment bundles two distinct challenges. The first is about scope: why does the calibration touch only the fixed-effect block \(\beta_{\text{FE}} = (\beta_1,\beta^W,\beta^B)^\top\in\mathbb R^p\) and leave the variance components \((\sigma^2_{\text{school}},\sigma^2)\) exactly as the sampler produced them? The second is about method: granted we are calibrating something, why a deterministic Cholesky reshape rather than importance-resampling the joint posterior \(\psi\)? I answer both, in order.
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

title: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas
text: C5: fixing the variance components? why not do importance resampling for the joint distribution instead of the conditional/profile version?
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

title: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas
text:
6.2 The CCC operator
The Cholesky Calibration Correction (CCC) is a deterministic, one-shot affine map on a draw cloud. For draws \(\{\beta_s\}_{s=1}^S\subset\mathbb R^p\), a target center \(c\in\mathbb R^p\), and an SPD target covariance \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\in\mathbb R^{p\times p}\), \[
\beta_s^{\text{cal}} = c + A\,(\beta_s - \bar\beta^{\text{raw}}), \qquad A = L_{\text{target}}\,L_{\text{raw}}^{-1},
\] where \(\bar\beta^{\text{raw}} = S^{-1}\sum_s\beta_s\) and \(L_{\text{raw}}, L_{\text{target}}\) are the lower-triangular Cholesky factors of the raw empirical covariance \(\Sigma_{\text{raw}}\) and of \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\) (\(L L^\top = \Sigma\)).
The R trap. Base R chol(Σ) returns the upper-triangular factor \(U\) with \(U^\top U = \Sigma\); the lower factor required here is t(chol(Σ)). Getting this wrong silently transposes \(A\) and miscalibrates the cloud in a way that survives a casual eye-check but fails the matrix residual diagnostic below.
Deterministic, unlike IWMM. Importance-weighted moment matching (IWMM) corrects moments by iteratively reweighting draws until the weighted mean and covariance converge, accumulating stochastic noise along the way. CCC closes in a single pass — factor \(\Sigma_{\text{raw}}\) and \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\) once, apply the affine map, done. No iteration, no convergence criterion, no random restart. That determinism is a genuine practical advantage when the same operator must run inside every pipeline.
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

title: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas
text:
6.3 Three invariants
The map satisfies three invariants by construction.

Mean. \(\operatorname{mean}\{\beta_s^{\text{cal}}\} = c\): centering at \(-\bar\beta^{\text{raw}}\) then shifting by \(+c\) places the centroid exactly at \(c\).
Covariance. \(\operatorname{Cov}\{\beta_s^{\text{cal}}\} = \Sigma_{\text{target}}\) to machine precision, since \(A\Sigma_{\text{raw}}A^\top = L_{\text{target}}L_{\text{raw}}^{-1}\Sigma_{\text{raw}}(L_{\text{raw}}^{-1})^\top L_{\text{target}}^\top = L_{\text{target}}L_{\text{target}}^\top = \Sigma_{\text{target}}\). A hand implementation in this project’s de-risk verified the residual \(\|A\Sigma_{\text{raw}}A^\top - \Sigma_{\text{target}}\|_{\max} \approx 4\times 10^{-17}\) in double precision. Two qualifications: under PSIS weights (Pipeline B) it is the weighted covariance that equals \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\) exactly, not the equal-weight one; and if the nearest-PD repair fires, the identity holds against the repaired \(\Sigma_{\text{raw}}\).
Block. The map acts on \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) only; the variance-component draws are appended unchanged (in code, .ccc_block_expand() places \(A\) in the FE block and the identity in the VC block).

A precision caveat, stated honestly. Invariants 1–2 match the first two moments exactly. CCC does not claim to correct the posterior beyond mean and covariance: the affine map carries higher-order structure of the raw cloud through linearly — skewness and kurtosis are linearly rescaled, not removed. CCC is therefore an exact moment-matching operation, and an exact distributional correction only when the raw draws are Gaussian. For the linear-Gaussian \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) block under a flat prior that is the relevant case (Section 3.1), but the limitation is worth naming.
The MCMC boundary, stated honestly. In exactly that linear-Gaussian case, the C-Direct deliverable is itself closed-form: \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) is a GLS solve and \(T_{\text{MI}}\) a BRR-Fay assembly, and the CCC reshapes any input cloud to those two moments. One could report \((\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}},
T_{\text{MI}})\) with no sampler at all — as could the orthodox path, since here the two coincide by Theorem 2.2. Far from a weakness, that is why this case is the right place to verify the machinery: an exact answer exists to check against (Section 10.1). The stacked MCMC earns its keep where the framework is actually aimed: nonlinear links (logistic or ordinal item-response models), genuinely joint variance-component inference, and non-Gaussian posteriors — settings with no closed-form \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) or \(T_{\text{MI}}\), where the one-fit-plus-CCC route is the only practical one and the orthodox alternative is not a formula but ten MCMC fits. The “one MCMC, not ten” economy is what that general case buys; the linear-Gaussian demonstration is what proves the single fit lands in the right place.
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

text:
6.4 Per-pipeline binding
The operator is pipeline-independent; only its three arguments change.









Pipeline

center \(c\)
target \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\)




A (orthodox)
\(M\) (per PV)
\(\hat\beta_m\)
\(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\)


B (stacked-PSIS)
\(M\) (PSIS-weighted)
\(\hat\beta_m^{\text{PSIS}} = \sum_s\tilde w_{m,s}\beta_s\)
\(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\)


C-Direct

\(T_{\text{MI}} = \bar U + (1+1/M)B\)



The \(T_{\text{MI}}\) caution. \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is a pre-pooling CCC target only for C-Direct, which calibrates a single pooled cloud. For A and B the per-PV target is \(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\); injecting \(T_{\text{MI}}\) per PV would over-disperse each cloud by \((1+1/M)B\), since the between-imputation term \(B\) is a property of the Rubin pooling step, not of any single PV’s posterior. In A and B, \(T_{\text{MI}}\) emerges post-pooling.
The center is external, by construction. In the deployed code C-Direct’s center \(c\) is the numeric \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — the BRR-Fay GLS pooled estimate, passed in as an external vector, not the raw stacked posterior mean. So the center column above is literal: because the map places the calibrated centroid at \(c\) regardless of where the raw cloud sits (invariant 1), C-Direct’s reported point equals Pipeline A’s exactly, and the R3a→R3b distance between the stacked posterior mean and \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) becomes a verification diagnostic, never carried into the deliverable (Section 10.1, Section 13.1).
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

title: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas
text:
6.5 Why only \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\): three compounding reasons
1 — Theorem 2.2 supplies an exact target only for \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\). The identity of Section 3.1 rests on the GLS gradient being linear in \(y^{(m)}\), which makes the stacked-bridge mode for \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) exactly \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — a closed-form calibration center. The profile score for \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\) carries a \(-\tfrac12\log\det V\) term and a quadratic in \(y^{(m)}\), both nonlinear; “average the per-PV scores” is no longer “evaluate at \(\bar y\),” and no clean Rubin-pool anchor results. The stacked mode for \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\) drifts from the Rubin-averaged value by a small amount — on the order of a percent or two at PISA scale, which we report in Part IV rather than assume (consistent with the R3a/R3b honesty of Section 3.1). A calibration whose own anchor is approximate cannot ground a precise CCC step.
2 — No design-based variance-component target is built in this paper. The CCC targets for \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) — \(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\) and \(T_{\text{MI}}\) — come from the BRR-Fay loop of Section 5.1, which is design-valid because \(\hat\beta_m\) is (conceptually) linear in the weights. A parallel BRR-Fay loop on variance components — forming a replicate sandwich of \(\hat\sigma^2_{\text{school},m}\) — would need its own replicate-REML routine and raises identification questions under PV stacking. That is the scope of the follow-up paper (the Williams–McGuire–Savitsky variance-component framework), not this one. Without a design-based VC target, there is nothing for a VC-CCC step to match.
3 — Support preservation: the structural safety reason. This is the strongest of the three and it is structural, not epistemic. \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\in\mathbb R^p\) is unconstrained, so the affine map is valid everywhere — it cannot move a draw out of the parameter space. Variance components obey \(\sigma^2,\sigma^2_{\text{school}}>0\); their support is the positive orthant, and an affine map applied to draws near the boundary can push them negative, violating the constraint — no choice of target rescues an affine reshape on the natural scale. (A variance-component calibration would instead work on an unconstrained reparameterization such as \(\log\sigma^2\) and exponentiate back — exactly the kind of extra machinery we defer to the follow-up paper.) On the natural scale, the CCC operator and variance-component geometry are simply incompatible.
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

title: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas
section: 6.6 Why not jointly importance-resample the whole vector?
text:
6.6 Why not jointly importance-resample the whole vector?
Your alternative — importance-resample \(\psi = (\beta_{\text{FE}},\sigma^2_{\text{school}},\sigma^2)\) jointly — is coherent, and it is worth being precise about what it would mean.
First, CCC is not resampling. Every draw the sampler produced appears in the calibrated output, with no thinning and no duplication; it is a reshape, not a redraw. Importance resampling instead draws a new sample with probabilities proportional to weights, introducing discrete approximation error and discarding low-weight draws.
Second, joint importance resampling of \(\psi\) over the \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) block is exactly what Pipeline B does (Section 7.1): the stacked bridge \(q_{\text{SWL}}\) is the proposal, the per-PV likelihood ratio \(L_m^0(\beta_s)/q_{\text{SWL}}(\beta_s)\) gives the weights, and PSIS stabilizes the tails. So the rigorous form of “use importance resampling” is already implemented. The real question is whether to extend it to the variance-component directions — and here the heavy-tail risk you raised in C1 bites directly. The bridge is, by Theorem 2.2, a good proposal for \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) (its mode sits at \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\), where the per-PV posteriors concentrate). For \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\) there is no such alignment guarantee: in a high-ICC setting (Korea’s mathematics ICC \(\approx 0.45\); the reading data analyzed in Section 12.1 is milder) the marginal can be sharply peaked with a heavy right tail, and the all-PV bridge can misplace its mass relative to a single PV’s posterior — exactly the high-\(\hat k\), unreliable-PSIS regime. So extending joint IS to the VC directions would walk into the tail problem without the mode-alignment that keeps \(\hat k\) manageable in the \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) directions.
The resulting strategy is therefore not a restriction of joint IS but the joint strategy with each block given its correct treatment:

\(\beta_{\text{FE}}\): deterministic CCC by default (A, C-Direct); Pipeline B adds PSIS resampling in these directions with a Pareto-\(\hat k\) gate — preferred when \(\hat k_m<0.7\), falling back to C-Direct otherwise.
\((\sigma^2_{\text{school}},\sigma^2)\): identity passthrough here; design-based VC calibration, including the joint-IS question, is the follow-up paper.
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

title: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas
text:
6.7 Weighted CCC for Pipeline B
When Pipeline B supplies normalized PSIS weights \(\{\tilde w_{m,s}\}\) (\(\sum_s\tilde w_{m,s}=1\)), the raw moments become their weighted counterparts, \[
\bar\beta^{\text{raw}} = \sum_s \tilde w_{m,s}\,\beta_s,
\qquad
\Sigma_{\text{raw}} = \sum_s \tilde w_{m,s}\,(\beta_s-\bar\beta^{\text{raw}})(\beta_s-\bar\beta^{\text{raw}})^\top,
\] the self-normalized weighted covariance the engine actually computes (the weights sum to one; the engine uses this convention deliberately rather than a Bessel-corrected estimator, the difference being \(O(\sum_s\tilde w_{m,s}^2)\) — negligible at the draw counts used). Should \(\Sigma_{\text{raw}}\) come out numerically non-positive-definite, a conditional nearest-PD repair (an eigenvalue floor at \(\varepsilon\cdot\operatorname{tr}(\Sigma_{\text{raw}})/p\) with \(\varepsilon = 10^{-10}\)) is applied before the Cholesky — it fires only when needed, not as an unconditional ridge. The per-draw map is unchanged; only the ingredients of \(A\) reflect the reweighting. Unweighted CCC (A, C-Direct) is the special case \(\tilde w_{m,s}=1/S\). In code a single draw_weights argument to engine_ccc() covers both (Section 8.1).
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

title: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas
text:
6.8 Summary
CCC is deterministic, non-iterative, and exact on \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) in the moment sense — mean \(c\) and covariance \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\) to machine precision — though not a full distributional correction beyond Gaussianity. The restriction to \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) compounds three justifications: Theorem 2.2 gives an exact anchor only there; no design-based VC target is built in this paper; and the affine map is structurally unsafe on positivity-constrained parameters. Joint importance resampling is available as Pipeline B for the \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) directions, with a Pareto-\(\hat k\) diagnostic (Section 7.1); the variance-component directions are the follow-up paper. The code is ccc_twolevel() / engine_ccc() (Section 8.1).
crumbs: 6  The Cholesky Calibration Correction, and Why Only the Betas

text:

text:
