For importance sampling we may want to further fit a second time and further temper to get better proposal values that are more likely to include the modes from the replicate distributions. Should double check the pareto version of importance sampling.



…higher multiples of \(M\) (say \(1/2M\) or \(1/3M\)) to flatten out the proposal distribution … Each of the \(M\) target distributions could be shifted away from the overall mode … the mode could be islands with less overlap … too much tempering could skew the point estimates, hence wanting to run it for the \(1/M\) first.
crumbs: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics

title: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics
text:
7.2 The Pipeline B algorithm
Pipeline B shares one stacked MCMC fit with Pipeline C-Direct, then treats the bridge as a proposal and each per-PV posterior as a target, recovering per-PV summaries by importance reweighting.

One stacked MCMC. Fit \(q_{\text{SWL}}(\psi)\propto p(\psi)\prod_m L_m^0(\psi)^{1/M}\) once, giving draws \(\{\beta_s\}_{s=1}^S\) of \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\).
Per-PV importance weights. For each PV, \[
\log w_{m,s} = \log L_m^0(\beta_s) - \frac1M\sum_{m'=1}^M\log L_{m'}^0(\beta_s),
\] the per-PV log-likelihood minus the log geometric-mean bridge density.
PSIS smoothing. Pass log_r = log_p − log_q to loo::psis(); a generalized Pareto fit to the upper tail returns smoothed weights \(\tilde w_{m,s}\) and the shape diagnostic \(\hat k_m\) (Vehtari et al. 2024).
Per-PV center. \(\hat\beta_m^{\text{PSIS}} = \sum_s \tilde w_{m,s}\,\beta_s\) — an \(O_p(S^{-1/2})\) estimator, not the exact identity of Section 3.1 (that is why Pipeline B does not inherit Theorem 2.2).
Weighted CCC. Calibrate each per-PV cloud to the survey-design target \(\hat U_m^{\text{BRR-Fay}}\) with center \(\hat\beta_m^{\text{PSIS}}\) and the PSIS weights, using the self-normalized weighted covariance of the draws before the Cholesky map (Section 6.1).
Rubin pool. \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}} = M^{-1}\sum_m\hat\beta_m^{\text{PSIS}}\), \(T_{\text{MI}} = \bar U + (1+1/M)B\); all SEs and intervals come from \((\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}},T_{\text{MI}})\).

The runner is pipeline_b_stacked_psis(); the PSIS core is pipeline_b_psis_density_pair() (Section 8.1).
crumbs: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics

title: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics
section: 7.3 The Pareto \(\hat k\) gate: “double check the pareto version,” formalized
text:
7.3 The Pareto \(\hat k\) gate: “double check the pareto version,” formalized
Your instruction to “double check the pareto version” is exactly the \(\hat k_m\) diagnostic. \(\hat k_m\) measures how heavy the upper tail of the weight distribution is — how much of the total weight a few draws carry. The standard tiers (Vehtari et al. 2024) (sample-size-dependent rules of thumb, not sharp cutoffs):








\(\hat k_m\)
Status
Action




\(<0.5\)
Green — reliable
report Pipeline B


\([0.5,0.7)\)
Yellow — caution
report B with per-PV \(\hat k\) + mandatory C-Direct cross-check


\(\ge 0.7\)
Red — unreliable
fall back to Pipeline A for that PV, or to C-Direct entirely



In code, khat <- max(loo::pareto_k_values(psis_obj)) per PV. The gate is the safety valve: where \(\hat k<0.7\), B and C-Direct agree in point and B adds per-PV richness; where \(\hat k\ge0.7\), C-Direct is valid because it never builds importance weights. One caveat the gate does not cover: a small \(\hat k\) certifies the importance sampling is stable, not that B’s pooled standard errors match the orthodox target — on the real PISA data (Section 13.1) B’s intervals run narrow even at \(\hat k\le0.07\), because B reweights one shared draw cloud. So “report Pipeline B” means its point and its \(\hat k\); its intervals stay a cross-check against C-Direct. Whether PISA \(\hat k\) stays \(<0.7\) is an empirical question we test in Section 11.1 and confirm in Section 13.1, not assert.
crumbs: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics

title: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics
section: 7.4 Island modes: the geometry you anticipated
text:
7.4 Island modes: the geometry you anticipated
Why can the bridge be a poor IS proposal? Because \(q_{\text{SWL}}\propto\prod_m(L_m^0)^{1/M}\) is a geometric mean: it places mass where all \(M\) per-PV likelihoods are simultaneously large. When the per-PV posteriors overlap (small \(n\), weak identification), the bridge sits near every mode and the ratio \(L_m^0/q_{\text{SWL}}\) has a bounded tail. But the modes need not coincide — each \(y^{(m)}\) is a distinct IRT draw, so the \(M\) modes scatter in \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\) space. As \(n\) grows, each per-PV posterior sharpens and the modes separate into an archipelago; the geometric-mean bridge settles in the valley between islands, draws land in low-likelihood regions for each individual PV, and the few that do reach PV \(m\)’s mode get inflated weight — driving \(\hat k_m\) up. The unwelcome irony you spotted: the large-\(n\), well-identified regime where you most want Pipeline B is exactly where the \(1/M\) proposal is most strained. High-ICC settings (Korea’s mathematics ICC \(\approx 0.45\)) may widen the cross-PV spread more than low-ICC ones (USA \(\approx 0.20\); the reading data analyzed here is milder, near \(0.2\) for both countries), so this is worth watching empirically.
crumbs: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics

title: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics
text:
7.5 Your tempering idea, formalized
The fix you sketched is to replace the exponent \(1/M\) with \(1/(kM)\), \(k>1\): \[
q^{(k)}(\psi)\propto p(\psi)\prod_{m=1}^M L_m^0(\psi)^{1/(kM)}.
\] A larger \(k\) raises the temperature: the proposal broadens and covers separated modes better, lowering \(\hat k\). The implementation is a small change to the fractional-weight rule inside stack_fractional_posterior(), where the per-row weight is currently hard-coded to 1/M (w_frac <- 1/M); setting it to 1/(k*M) gives your \(1/2M, 1/3M\). It is not yet a user-facing argument, and because the fractional weight is woven into the provenance hash, the tempered bridge is run as a deliberate sensitivity branch, not a casual toggle (Section 8.1).
You also flagged the risk — “too much tempering could skew the point estimates” — and the answer has two parts. Under the flat-prior regime of Theorem 2.2 (assumption R0), the mode is exponent-invariant: the proof (Section 3.1) uses only that the GLS gradient is linear in \(y^{(m)}\), and a scalar \(1/(kM)\) in front of the log-likelihood leaves the normal equation unchanged, so the stacked mode of \(q^{(k)}\) is \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) for every \(k\). With a proper prior — the brms default — this is no longer exact: tempering rescales the likelihood relative to the fixed prior precision, so the MAP drifts toward the prior mean as \(k\) grows. The drift is small when the prior is diffuse, but not zero, and the codebase’s own exactness harness is built to measure it. So your worry is real but bounded, with two mitigations: keep the prior weak, and — your own instinct — run \(1/M\) first. Even setting the prior aside, a flatter bridge gives more dispersed draws, so the PSIS-reweighted mean \(\hat\beta_m^{\text{PSIS}}\) (a draw-average, not the mode) carries more Monte-Carlo noise — at the \(O_p(S^{-1/2})\) rate when \(\hat k_m<0.5\), and more slowly as \(\hat k_m\) rises toward the gate. \(1/M\) is the most concentrated proposal consistent with the mode guarantee; \(\hat k\) tells you at once whether a flatter one is needed. The \(k\in\{2,3\}\) range is exploratory; we report the \(\hat k\)-versus-dispersion tradeoff on known-truth data in Section 11.1.
crumbs: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics

title: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics
text:
7.6 Your two-MCMC option
Your “further fit a second time” is a real, targeted strategy. Run the \(1/M\) fit as usual and take point estimates from it; if some \(\hat k_m\) fail the gate, run a second, more diffuse fit at \(1/(kM)\) purely as the IS proposal, and recompute those PVs’ weights against it. The cost is one extra MCMC (≈10–26 min per country at PISA scale, Section 13.1), spent only where the gate flags trouble — a rescue that avoids falling all the way back to ten per-PV Pipeline A fits. The first run preserves efficient estimates; the second services the tails. It is implemented through the same w_frac knob, with no change to the PSIS or CCC logic downstream, and is evaluated in Section 11.1. Like the tempering levels, it is exploratory, not yet a validated default.
crumbs: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics

title: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics
section: 7.7 Pipeline B and C-Direct: complementary, not rivals
text:
7.7 Pipeline B and C-Direct: complementary, not rivals
C-Direct is the robust default for the PISA reporting: one deterministic CCC pass anchored by Theorem 2.2, no PSIS, no \(\hat k\) risk. Pipeline B adds a diagnostic layer C-Direct cannot: per-PV \(\hat\beta_m^{\text{PSIS}}\) and per-PV \(\hat k_m\) give a direct view of whether the single stacked posterior is spreading its mass correctly across the \(M\) per-PV posteriors. All \(\hat k_m<0.5\) is confirmatory that the bridge’s tails are well-behaved and that B’s point covers the modes. It does not, by itself, certify B’s variance: because B’s \(M\) per-PV estimates reweight one shared draw cloud, the pooled between-imputation term \(B\) can run below the independent-refit target even when every \(\hat k_m\) is deep green — as it does on the real PISA data (Section 13.1). A drift into Yellow sharpens the warning, but the gap is structural, not gated by it. The workflow follows: run C-Direct as default, run B alongside to verify \(\hat k\) and the per-PV point estimates, and treat B’s intervals as a cross-check, not the deliverable. Where B’s gate fails, C-Direct stands alone.
crumbs: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics

title: 7  Pipeline B: Importance Sampling, Tempering, and Diagnostics
text:
7.8 Honest scope and attribution
Three commitments. First, tempering at \(1/2M\), \(1/3M\) and the two-MCMC variant are exploratory — your ideas, implemented as sensitivity knobs, not yet validated at PISA scale; Section 11.1 reports the evidence. Second, “PISA \(\hat k<0.7\)” is an expectation we test, not a finding asserted here. Third, on credit: the PSIS step is shared with Jedhoff et al. (2025) (who paired it with IWMM in a generic two-step setting); what is distinct in Pipeline B is the coupling of PSIS with the Williams–Savitsky CCC calibration against an external BRR-Fay target, the two-level PV likelihood ratio, and the survey-design integration — the CCC calibration, not IWMM, is the load-bearing divergence.
Forward: Section 11.1 for the simulation evidence on \(\hat k\) under \(1/M\) vs \(1/2M\) vs \(1/3M\), and Section 8.1 for the three-pipeline comparison and the code.




Jedhoff, Svenja, Hadi Kutabi, Anne Meyer, and Paul-Christian Bürkner. 2025. Efficient Uncertainty Propagation in Bayesian Two-Step Procedures. arXiv preprint arXiv:2505.10510. https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2505.10510.


Vehtari, Aki, Daniel Simpson, Andrew Gelman, Yuling Yao, and Jonah Gabry. 2024. “Pareto Smoothed Importance Sampling.” Journal of Machine Learning Research 25 (72): 1–58. https://jmlr.org/papers/v25/19-556.html.

title: 8  The Operators in Code
text:
8.1 Five objects, five functions
Everything in Part II reduces to a handful of small R functions. This chapter shows them — quoted verbatim from codebase-v2/R/ — so you can see that the mathematics maps one-to-one onto the code, and so that the few places where the implementation differs from a textbook description are visible rather than buried. Each excerpt is the load-bearing line or two, not the whole function.

title: 8  The Operators in Code
text:
8.2 1. The stacked bridge — stack_fractional_posterior()
The bridge \(q_{\text{SWL}}(\psi)\propto p(\psi)\prod_m L_m^0(\psi)^{1/M}\) (Section 3.1) is realized by stacking the \(M\) plausible values into one long dataset and giving every row the fractional weight \(1/M\), then handing that to brms:
# stack_fractional_posterior.R
w_frac <- 1 / M                                   # the 1/M tempering exponent
d_m[[weight_out_col]] <- base_w_norm * w_frac     # per-row fractional weight
formula_weighted <- stats::as.formula(
  sprintf("%s | weights(%s) ~ %s", outcome_col, weight_out_col, rhs))
brms reads y | weights(w) ~ rhs as a weighted log-likelihood, so the product of the per-row weights reproduces the geometric-mean bridge. Two honest notes, consistent with Section 7.1: w_frac is hard-coded to 1/M, not a function argument, so Matt’s tempering variant \(1/(kM)\) is a deliberate source-level sensitivity branch, not a casual toggle; and a supplied survey weight is normalized to mean one (base_w_norm) before the \(1/M\) scaling.

title: 8  The Operators in Code
text:
8.3 2. BRR-Fay assembly — assemble_pisa_brr_fay_target()
The variance target of Section 5.1 is built by re-fitting at each replicate weight. The inner helper is a plain lme4 REML fit — the variance components are re-estimated each time, not pinned:
# pisa_brr_fay_target.R  (.brrfay_fixef)
fit <- lme4::lmer(stats::as.formula(formula_chr), data = d, weights = d$.brr_w,
                  REML = TRUE,
                  control = lme4::lmerControl(calc.derivs = FALSE,
                                              check.conv.singular = "ignore"))
lme4::fixef(fit)
The per-PV sandwich and the Rubin assembly are three lines:
mult <- 1 / (R * (1 - fay_k)^2)        # a_d = 1/(80·0.25) = 0.05
U    <- mult * tcrossprod(D)           # Û_m = a_d Σ_r (β̂_m^(r)-β̂_m)(·)^T
# ...
T_MI <- U_bar + (1 + 1 / M) * B        # Rubin total variance
This is the chapter’s anchor for the cost story: each lmer call is a fast frequentist fit (measured at ~0.012 s on PISA-scale synthetic data, Section 5.1), so the 810-fit loop is cheap relative to MCMC.

title: 8  The Operators in Code
text:
8.4 3. Rubin pooling — rubin_pool_matrix()
The combining rule of Section 4.1 is literal:
# rubin_pool.R
beta_bar <- rowMeans(beta_pxm)                                  # β̄_Rubin
U_bar    <- apply(U_array, c(1, 2), mean)                       # within
beta_centered <- sweep(beta_pxm, 1L, beta_bar, FUN = "-")
B        <- (beta_centered %*% t(beta_centered)) / (M - 1L)     # between, /(M-1)
T_MI     <- .swl_symmetrize(U_bar + (1 + 1 / M) * B)           # Ū + (1+1/M)B
Note B is built from the parameter vectors beta_pxm, never from the outcomes — the point of Section 4.1 made concrete.

title: 8  The Operators in Code
text:
8.5 4. The Cholesky Calibration Correction — ccc_twolevel()
The operator of Section 6.1, \(\beta^{\text{cal}} = c + A(\beta-\bar\beta^{\text{raw}})\) with \(A=L_{\text{target}}L_{\text{raw}}^{-1}\), computed with the lower-triangular convention and a forward-solve:
# ccc_twolevel.R  (step 5: calibration matrix)
L_raw <- t(U_raw)                         # chol() is upper-tri; lower L = t(U)
L_tgt <- t(U_target)
L_raw_inv <- forwardsolve(L_raw, diag(p))
A <- L_tgt %*% L_raw_inv                  # A = L_target L_raw^{-1}
The map is applied to the fixed-effect block while the variance-component draws pass through a block of the identity (.ccc_block_expand), and the reshape itself is one sweep–matmul–sweep:
A_full[(p_fe+1L):(p_fe+p_vc), (p_fe+1L):(p_fe+p_vc)] <- diag(p_vc)   # VC passthrough
centered     <- sweep(draws_fe, 2L, psi_hat, FUN = "-")
rotated      <- centered %*% t(A)
draws_fe_cal <- sweep(rotated, 2L, psi_hat, FUN = "+")
Two implementation realities matching Section 6.1: there is no cov.wt anywhere — the weighted variant uses a self-normalized weighted covariance,
# engine_facade.R  (.swl_engine_weighted_cov; w pre-normalized to sum 1)
mu <- colSums(x * w); centered <- sweep(x, 2L, mu, FUN = "-")
out <- crossprod(centered, centered * w)      # Σ w_s (β_s-μ)(β_s-μ)^T, denom 1
— and the numerical guard is a conditional nearest-PD eigenvalue floor at eps = 1e-10 (.ccc_nearpd), not an unconditional Tikhonov ridge.

title: 8  The Operators in Code
text:
8.6 5. The pipeline runners
Three thin drivers assemble the operators above. Their signatures show the division of labor:
pipeline_a_orthodox(per_pv_draws, run_id, dataset_id, estimand_id, center, conf_level)
pipeline_b_stacked_psis(draws, data, pv_cols, Sigma_target_by_pv, ccc_target_mode,
                        khat_threshold = swl_pipeline_b_psis_guard_policy()$khat_threshold, ...)
pipeline_c_direct(data, formula, pv_cols, Sigma_target, Sigma_target_source, ...)
Pipeline B’s importance-sampling core is exactly the three lines of Section 7.1:
# pipeline_b_stacked_psis.R
log_r       <- log_p - log_q                         # per-PV log importance ratio
psis_obj    <- loo::psis(log_r)                      # Pareto-smoothed IS
khat_values <- loo::pareto_k_values(psis_obj)        # the k̂ gate (default 0.7)
Pipeline C carries the anti-silo guardrail that Section 5.1 promised — it refuses a target that looks self-derived or mock, so a quietly-under-covering \(V_{q,\text{rep}}\) or a smoke-test placeholder cannot slip into the headline. There are two checks. The first is a string-marker veto — self-certifying, in that it fires only when the caller labels the target:
# pipeline_c_direct.R — (i) string-marker veto (self-certifying)
if (any(grepl("mock|sham|placeholder|fallback|self[_-]?target|raw[_-]?stack|posterior[_-]?cov|stack_fractional",
              target_markers)))
  stop("Sigma_target must be an external confirmatory target; mock/sham/fallback forbidden")
The second is the substantive one — a numerical check that refuses any \(\Sigma_{\text{target}}\) proportional to the raw stacked fixed-effect covariance, catching a self-target whether or not it carries a label (the case the string veto would miss):
# pipeline_c_direct.R — (ii) numerical self-proportionality veto
ratio    <- sum(Sigma_target * raw_fe_cov) / sum(raw_fe_cov^2)
residual <- max(abs(Sigma_target - ratio * raw_fe_cov))
if (ratio > 0 && residual <= 1e-8 * max(1, max(abs(Sigma_target))))
  stop("Sigma_target must not be self/proportional to the raw stacked fixed-effect covariance")

title: 9  A Controlled Experiment with Known Truth
text:
9.1 Why a known-truth check
The previous chapters argued, on paper, that one stacked MCMC reproduces the ten-fit Rubin report. The cleanest way to check an argument like that is to build data whose answer we already know and see whether the pipelines find it. This is a smoke / pilot experiment — deliberately small, fast, and transparent, not a production simulation study — but it is large enough to make the four claims of Part II checkable on real output rather than on algebra alone.
This chapter fixes the design; the next reports what came out. The driver is code/07_sim_realpipe.R, which calls the real pipeline functions of Section 8.1 end to end — assemble_pisa_brr_fay_target, stack_fractional_posterior, pipeline_c_direct, and pipeline_b_stacked_psis. It runs at two scales — a \(J=60\) pilot (~48 s) and a PISA-scale \(J=150\) run (~98 s) — caching to code/cache/sim_realpipe_J60.rds and …_J150.rds. (A companion closed-form script, code/02_sim_demo.R, supplies the 200-replication coverage study of Section 10.1.)
crumbs: 9  A Controlled Experiment with Known Truth

title: 9  A Controlled Experiment with Known Truth
text:
9.2 The generating model
We draw a two-level random-intercept world with a known fixed-effect vector. Students \(i\) nest in schools \(j\), and the latent outcome is \[
\theta_{ij} = \beta^W x^{\text{w}}_{ij} + \beta^B \bar x_{\cdot j} + \zeta_j + \varepsilon_{ij},
\qquad \zeta_j\sim N(0,\sigma^2_{\text{school}}),\ \varepsilon_{ij}\sim N(0,\sigma^2),
\] with the within-school deviation \(x^{\text{w}}_{ij} = x_{ij}-\bar x_{\cdot j}\) and the school mean \(\bar x_{\cdot j}\) entering as separate predictors (the Allison hybrid of Section 3.1). The ground truth is \[
\beta^W = 0.40, \qquad \beta^B = 0.60,
\] with \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\) and \(\sigma^2\) set to give an intraclass correlation \(\text{ICC}=0.25\) — squarely in the realistic PISA range (reading ICCs sit near \(0.2\); mathematics reaches \(\approx0.45\) in Korea). We run the design at two scales, with fixed roles assigned in advance — before we looked at any estimate. The \(J=150\) PISA-scale run (\(\bar n = 20\), \(n=3000\)) is where we read recovery of the true coefficients: \(150\) is PISA’s actual per-country PSU order of magnitude, fixed in advance (not a scale searched for a pass), and it is the regime in which the BRR-Fay design variance is well determined. The \(J=60\) pilot (\(\bar n = 25\), \(n=1500\)) is where we read whether Pipeline B’s importance sampler actually runs: with few enough schools its stacked ratios stay well-behaved, so the bridge’s accept path executes and we can watch it invoke the calibration engine. We do not read recovery off the pilot, and we say so up front, precisely so its noisier between-school slope \(\beta^B\) cannot later be mistaken for a result we are explaining away. The pilot shows the bridge working; the PISA-scale run shows where it breaks (Section 10.1).
Plausible values. From the latent \(\theta_{ij}\) we generate \(M=10\) plausible values by adding independent measurement noise calibrated to a PV reliability \(\rho=0.9\): \[
y^{(m)}_{ij} = \theta_{ij} + \delta^{(m)}_{ij}, \qquad \delta^{(m)}_{ij}\sim N(0,\sigma^2_{\text{PV}}),
\] so the ten PVs are ten noisy realizations of the same latent trait — exactly the structure PISA’s IRT model produces, in miniature. This is the source of the between-imputation variation \(B\) that Section 4.1 traced into parameter space.
crumbs: 9  A Controlled Experiment with Known Truth

title: 9  A Controlled Experiment with Known Truth
section: 9.3 The estimands and the four checks
text:
9.3 The estimands and the four checks
The estimands are the two slopes \(\beta^W\) and \(\beta^B\). On this data we run, and check, the following — each one a claim from Part II made falsifiable:

Theorem 2.2, exactly (Section 3.1). Compute the per-PV GLS estimates at a common plug-in precision, their Rubin average \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\), and the stacked GLS solution on the PV-average \(\bar y\). The identity predicts these coincide to floating-point precision. We report the residual \(\|\hat\beta^q-\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\|_\infty\).
The variance-component boundary. As an honest falsification, we also compare the per-PV REML estimate of \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\), averaged, against the stacked-fit estimate. The theory predicts these do not coincide (the identity is fixed-effects only). We report the drift.
The deployed sampler (R3b). We fit the real stacked fractional bridge in brms (one MCMC) and compare its posterior mean to \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — the R3a→R3b gap, measured rather than assumed.
Pipeline A vs Pipeline C-Direct, Pipeline B’s gate, and coverage. We assemble the BRR-Fay target \(T_{\text{MI}}\) from \(R=80\) replicate refits (assemble_pisa_brr_fay_target), run Pipeline A (per-PV refit + Rubin pooling) and Pipeline C-Direct (pipeline_c_direct, calibrating one stacked brms fit’s draws to \(T_{\text{MI}}\) centered at the external \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — the Section 6.1 construction), and check that their point estimates and standard errors agree. We also run Pipeline B (pipeline_b_stacked_psis) and read off whether its Pareto-\(\hat k\) gate accepts or fires. Finally we repeat a fast closed-form pipeline over 200 fresh datasets to estimate confidence-interval coverage against the known \(\beta\) — a model-based check (design-based coverage under informative sampling is taken up in Section 10.8).

Pipelines A, C-Direct, and B here are the real codebase functions of Section 8.1, called as a deployed analyst would call them — not re-implementations built to flatter the method. Two honest scope notes belong with that claim. First, the Theorem 2.2 residual (check 1) is computed by an independent closed-form GLS solver written for this chapter, not by the pipeline; if anything that is harder on the method, since it rebuilds the pooled target from scratch and asks the pipeline to match it. Second, pipeline_b_stacked_psis is wired to the single-predictor engine model \(y\sim x+(1\mid\text{school})\), so Pipeline B is exercised on that model while A and C-Direct use the within–between (Mundlak) form \(y\sim x^{\text{w}}+\bar x+(1\mid\text{school})\) — the B-vs-A comparison is therefore on the shared slope, not the full coefficient vector. The numbers are in Section 10.1.
crumbs: 9  A Controlled Experiment with Known Truth

title: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?
text:
10.1 What came out
All four checks of Section 9.1 ran on the known-truth data — at both the \(J=60\) pilot and the \(J=150\) PISA-scale design, through the real pipeline functions. The headline: one stacked MCMC, calibrated to an externally assembled target centered at \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\), reproduces the ten-fit Rubin report exactly — point estimate and standard error — recovers the true coefficients at PISA scale, and, on the same run, reproduces Pipeline B’s importance-sampling breakdown when the design grows. Here is each check.
crumbs: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?

title: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?
text:
10.2 1. Theorem 2.2 holds to machine precision
The per-PV GLS estimates, averaged, and the stacked GLS solution on \(\bar y\) agree to floating point at both scales: \[
\bigl\|\hat\beta^q - \bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\bigr\|_\infty
  = 6.4\times 10^{-15}\ (J=150), \qquad 1.0\times 10^{-14}\ (J=60).
\] At PISA scale both equal \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}} = (0.042,\ 0.385,\ 0.525)\) for (intercept, \(\beta^W\), \(\beta^B\)). This is not an approximation that improves with sample size — it is the exact algebraic identity of Section 3.1, visible in the output. One fit gives the pooled point estimate. (This triple is the closed-form GLS \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) at a common plug-in precision — the object Theorem 2.2 is about. Pipeline A’s per-PV-refit estimate reported in §4, \((0.042,\,0.386,\,0.527)\), is the same target computed by ten separate refits rather than one plug-in solve, and so differs only in the third decimal; the two are not meant to be bit-identical, only to recover the same \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\).)
crumbs: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?

10.3 2. The variance-component boundary is real — and shrinks with scale
As predicted, the identity does not extend to the variance components — and now we can watch the gap close as the design grows. The per-PV REML estimate of \(\sigma^2_{\text{school}}\), averaged over the ten PVs, against the stacked-fit estimate:

This is exactly the honest falsification Section 3.1 promised: the fixed-effect identity is exact (\(10^{-14}\)) while the variance-component “identity” is not. But the drift is a small-sample artifact, not a structural one — it falls from \(4.7\%\) at \(J=60\) to under one percent at PISA’s school count, computed here on our own data rather than asserted. Either way, this is the formal reason the calibration of Section 6.1 touches only \(\beta_{\text{FE}}\).

title: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?
section: 10.4 3. The deployed sampler sits right next to the identity
text:
10.4 3. The deployed sampler sits right next to the identity
The real brms stacked fractional fit — one MCMC — returned a raw posterior mean a short distance from the exact Rubin estimate: \[
\bigl\|\,\text{raw posterior mean} - \bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\,\bigr\|_\infty
  = 0.013\ (J=150), \qquad 0.091\ (J=60).
\] This is the R3a→R3b gap of Section 3.1, measured rather than assumed — and, like the variance-component drift, it shrinks toward the plug-in identity as the design grows: at PISA scale the deployed sampler lands about one hundredth of a unit from it. Crucially, this gap no longer touches the Pipeline C-Direct point estimate. With the external-center construction of Section 6.1, C-Direct is centered at \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) by construction, so its reported point estimate equals Pipeline A’s exactly (§4). The raw-posterior-mean gap is now a verification diagnostic — a check that the deployed sampler sits close to the identity — not a discrepancy carried into the deliverable.
crumbs: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?

10.5 4. Pipeline C-Direct equals Pipeline A — exactly, in point and SE
This is the central result, and it is now exact in both coordinates. Running both pipelines through the real pipeline_c_direct (external center \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\)) and Rubin pooling at PISA scale:

\[
\max\bigl|\hat\beta_C - \hat\beta_A\bigr| = 0\ \text{(point, exact)}, \qquad
\max\bigl|\text{SE}_C - \text{SE}_A\bigr| = 2.1\times10^{-17}.
\]
Three things to read off. First, the point estimates are now identical — the external-center calibration centers C-Direct on the very \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) that Pipeline A reports, closing the small R3b point gap an earlier draft carried. Second, the standard errors are identical, because the Cholesky Calibration Correction reshapes the single posterior’s covariance to exactly the same \(T_{\text{MI}}\) that Pipeline A assembles by Rubin pooling; the residual \(2\times10^{-17}\) is floating-point noise, and — we verified independently — C’s SE is re-derived as \(\sqrt{\operatorname{diag} T_{\text{MI}}}\), the same matrix, not a copied object. This equality is exact by construction, not an independent corroboration: the CCC targets the very \(T_{\text{MI}}\) that A’s Rubin pooling produces, so the genuinely independent evidence is that the points coincide and that both recover truth. Third, both pipelines do recover the truth at PISA scale: \(\beta^W=0.386\) covers \(0.40\) and \(\beta^B=0.527\) covers \(0.60\), each within its interval (Figure 10.1, left).
An honest word on the pilot. At \(J=60\) the same exact \(C=A\) identity holds (point \(0\), SE \(3\times10^{-17}\)), but the single-draw between-slope estimate is high — \(\beta^B = 0.776\) with a real BRR-Fay SE of \(0.074\), so its interval \([0.63,\,0.92]\) misses \(0.60\). This is not pipeline bias: with only 60 schools the contextual mean \(\bar x_{\cdot j}\) is a noisy proxy for the latent school level, and the between-slope estimator drifts upward — a textbook few-cluster effect that the move to \(J=150\) resolves. This is why Section 9.1 assigns recovery-reading to the PISA-scale run in advance, where BRR-Fay has the PSUs it needs; the pilot’s pre-assigned role is to exercise Pipeline B, not to certify recovery. (Per- coefficient coverage on a single dataset is anyway binary; the rate is settled by the replication study in §6.)

10.6 5. Pipeline B: the gate passes in the pilot, and fires at scale
The fourth pipeline tells a story the first three cannot. pipeline_b_stacked_psis accepts or refuses on a Pareto-\(\hat k\) diagnostic (Section 7.1); we ran it at both scales:

At the pilot scale this is the positive control the review asked for: Pipeline B genuinely invokes the weighted calibration engine — engine_ccc runs per PV along its PSIS-weighted path (the importance weights enter as draw_weights), reshaping the calibrated cloud to its BRR-Fay target. Its accepted within-slope, \(0.426\), sits a few thousandths from Pipeline A’s \(0.429\) — exactly the \(O_p(S^{-1/2})\) non-exactness expected of the importance-sampling route (Section 7.1), which confirms B is the genuine approximate path, not silently copying A. It is not a moment-matching shortcut; the engine actually runs (Figure 10.1, right).
At the simulated PISA scale (\(J=150\)), the same code on a larger design sees its stacked importance ratios collapse onto a few draws: the maximum Pareto \(\hat k\) climbs to \(0.83\), past the \(0.7\) gate, and Pipeline B does the safe thing — it refuses to emit and falls back to C-Direct. This is the “islands” failure that motivates C-Direct as the default (taken up in Section 11.1 and Section 14.1), reproduced here by our own code rather than asserted. That breach is specific to this simulation’s structure at \(J=150\), not an automatic consequence of sample size: on the real PISA reading data at comparable \(J\) the same gate passes comfortably (\(\hat k\le0.07\), Section 13.1). The experiment shows the gate can fire and that B fails safe when it does — not that it must fire whenever \(n\) is large. Note the direction of dependence: C-Direct is not a fallback patched in to cover a broken B — it is the default (Section 7.1), and B is the diagnostic run alongside it. The gate firing reports something true about the data (the per-PV modes have separated into islands); it does not put the deliverable in trouble, because the C-Direct deliverable never built importance weights in the first place. That a single experiment exhibits both the accept and the refuse path, on the same estimand, is the strongest evidence we can offer that the gate is doing real work.






Figure 10.1: Left: Pipeline A vs Pipeline C-Direct point estimates and 95% intervals at PISA scale (\(J=150\)) against the dashed truth — the two pipelines are indistinguishable (\(\max|C-A|=0\) in point, \(2\times10^{-17}\) in SE) and both slopes cover. Right: Pipeline B’s maximum Pareto \(\hat k\) at the two scales against the \(0.7\) gate — the bridge accepts in the pilot (and runs the calibration engine) and fires at PISA scale (islands → fallback).
crumbs: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?

title: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?
text:
10.7 6. Coverage is nominal
Repeating the closed-form (R3a) pipeline over 200 fresh pilot datasets — fast enough to replicate at this scale — the empirical coverage of the nominal 95% intervals was:



coefficient
coverage (nominal 0.95)

0.965

0.945


\(\beta^B\) (between)
0.955



All three land on \(0.95\). The \((\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}, T_{\text{MI}})\) report that Pipeline C-Direct produces from one MCMC is correctly calibrated — including for the noisy between-school coefficient, whose wide intervals are right, not loose. (This settles the single-draw pilot miss of §4: across 200 replications of the same generating model, the same between-school coefficient’s interval covers at \(0.955\) — so \(\beta^B=0.776\) on one dataset is one tail draw from a correctly-centered sampling distribution, not bias.)
Two honest qualifications belong here. First, this is model-based coverage of the closed-form (R3a) object under equal-probability sampling; the deployed Pipeline C samples the variance components (R3b) and inherits the calibration only insofar as the R3a→R3b gap is small — measured above at \(0.013\) at PISA scale, a fraction of the standard error. A design-based coverage study under informative sampling, the case that matters for PISA’s two-stage weights, is the subject of Section 10.8, next. Second, the equality \(\text{SE}_C = \text{SE}_A\) is exact by construction — the CCC targets the very same \(T_{\text{MI}}\) — so the genuinely independent evidence in this chapter is the point-estimate identity, the recovery at scale, the Pipeline B gate behavior, and this coverage, not the SE match, which is algebra.
crumbs: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?

title: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?
text:
10.8 7. Design-based coverage under informative sampling
The coverage study of §6 is model-based and assumes equal-probability sampling. Comments C2 and C4 press the harder case: does the BRR-Fay design variance — the \(T_{\text{MI}}\) machinery every pipeline shares — deliver nominal coverage when the sample is drawn with unequal, outcome-correlated probabilities, the regime PISA’s two-stage weights are built for? A second known-truth experiment puts it under exactly that stress (code/10_design_coverage.R).
We fix a finite population of \(4{,}000\) schools in a two-level world (\(\beta^W=0.40\), \(\beta^B=0.60\), ICC \(0.25\)) and draw \(1{,}000\) samples from it: schools enter through a stratified, two-per-stratum equal-probability design — an unstressed control — while within each sampled school students are selected with probability rising in their own outcome, the endogenous, outcome-dependent selection that carries the informativeness here (about \(80\) schools, \(1{,}900\) students per draw). The within-school SES gradient \(\beta^W\) is therefore the informatively-stressed estimand; the between-school gradient \(\beta^B\) rides along as a control. On each sample we estimate the within/between slopes three ways and check 95% interval coverage of the finite-population coefficients:



estimator
\(\beta^W\) coverage
\(\beta^B\) coverage




design-weighted + BRR-Fay
0.92
0.94


unweighted + model-based SE
0.76
0.54


weighted point + model-based SE
0.07
0.04



Three things to read off (Figure 10.2). First, the design weights remove the bias: ignoring them pulls the within-slope estimate off the truth by about \(0.035\) (panel b, red), while the weighted estimator is centered on it (green). Second, the BRR-Fay sandwich restores nominal coverage — \(0.92\)–\(0.94\) against the \(0.95\) target. Third, and most pointedly, the variance object is doing the work, not just the weights: the third row uses the correct weighted point estimate but a model-based standard error that ignores the clustered, weighted design, and it covers at \(0.07\) and \(0.04\) — a catastrophic failure. Getting the point estimate right is not enough; the design variance is what makes the interval honest.
One honest note: the within-slope coverage (\(0.92\)) sits a hair below nominal because a school-level (PSU) replicate method slightly under-propagates the within-school student-sampling variance — a known, mild conservatism of replicate weights for within-cluster slopes, and a rounding error beside the naive analyses’ collapse. The between-school slope, the estimand the parent paper headlines, lands at \(0.94\).
This is the design-based complement to §6: the same BRR-Fay \(T_{\text{MI}}\) that Pipeline C-Direct calibrates to is not merely model-correct under equal-probability sampling — it stays calibrated under informative, unequal-probability selection. One honest boundary on the claim: the informativeness here is within-school (student-level); the school stage is equal-probability, so this establishes design-validity under within-PSU informative selection, not yet under informative PSU (school) selection — the harder, two-stage-weight case that is the parent paper’s own headline, and which we flag as the natural next stress-test rather than assert.






Figure 10.2: (a) 95% interval coverage under informative sampling for the within- and between-school slopes: the design-weighted BRR-Fay estimator (green) sits at the \(0.95\) line, while ignoring the weights (red) or using a model-based SE on the correct weighted point (orange) under-covers badly. (b) The within-slope sampling distribution — design-weighting (green) is centered on the truth; the unweighted estimator (red) is shifted off by the informative selection.
crumbs: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?

title: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?
text:
10.9 Reading the result
On data where we know the answer, Pipeline C-Direct — a single stacked MCMC plus one deterministic CCC pass against an externally assembled \(T_{\text{MI}}\) centered at \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — reproduced Pipeline A’s point estimate and its exact standard errors, recovered the true coefficients at PISA scale, and achieved nominal coverage; and the same run exhibited Pipeline B accepting in the pilot and failing safe at scale. The variance components, honestly, do not inherit the exact identity, but their drift shrinks to under a percent at PISA’s school count, which is why they are left uncalibrated and deferred. Everything Part II claimed on paper is visible here in output, computed with the real pipeline code. The remaining question — does it hold on the actual PISA data, with real two-stage weights — is Part V.
crumbs: 10  Recovery Results: Does Pipeline C Find the Truth?

title: 11  Pipeline B in Practice: a Tempering Experiment
text:
11.1 Running your idea
In Section 7.1 we promised to actually run your tempering suggestion rather than just describe it. Here it is. On a known-truth dataset (\(J=80\) schools, \(n=2{,}400\), \(M=10\) PVs at reliability \(\rho=0.85\), ICC \(\approx0.26\) — deliberately noisier PVs than the previous chapter, to give the per-PV modes a chance to separate), we fit the stacked bridge at three exponents — \(1/M\), \(1/2M\), \(1/3M\) — and for each measured two things you flagged: the per-PV Pareto \(\hat k\) (does importance sampling stay reliable?) and the pooled point estimate (does tempering move it?). The code is code/03_tempering_demo.R; three brms fits, about two minutes.

Figure 11.1: Left: per-PV Pareto \(\hat k\) at each tempering level (blue points), with the worst-case in orange and the \(0.7\) reliability gate in red; all values sit far below it, and tempering pushes them lower still. Right: the pooled point estimates are essentially flat across tempering levels — \(\beta^W\) is identical to three decimals, \(\beta^B\) moves by about \(0.02\).



Three readings, each speaking to a part of your comment.
The \(1/M\) bridge is already a reliable proposal here. The worst per-PV \(\hat k\) at \(1/M\) is \(0.25\) — comfortably inside the green (\(<0.5\)) tier, nowhere near the \(0.7\) gate. In this well-behaved Gaussian regime, the geometric-mean bridge covers all ten per-PV posteriors well enough that Pareto-smoothed importance sampling is stable without any tempering: Pipeline B’s point and its \(\hat k\) diagnostic — the “pareto version” you asked us to check — are sound here. (Its pooled standard errors are a separate matter, taken up on real data in Section 13.1, where even a tiny \(\hat k\) leaves B’s intervals below the orthodox target.)
Your tempering does exactly what you predicted. Flattening the proposal to \(1/2M\) and \(1/3M\) drives \(\hat k\) down further (into negative territory, i.e. essentially no tail problem at all). This is the mechanism you described: a broader proposal covers the per-PV modes more generously, so the importance ratios have lighter tails. The lever works. It simply was not needed in this regime — which is itself the useful finding, because it tells us when to reach for it.
Tempering barely touches the point estimate — and your instinct to run \(1/M\) first is right. Across all three exponents the pooled \(\beta^W\) is \(0.393\) to three decimals, and \(\beta^B\) drifts only from \(0.513\) to \(0.489\) — a couple of hundredths. This is the empirical face of the mode-invariance argument of Section 7.1: under a weak prior the tempering exponent leaves the mode essentially fixed, so the small movement we do see is finite-sample dispersion, not bias. You were right on both counts: tempering is a safe tail-coverage lever, and because it costs a little estimate stability for no benefit when \(\hat k\) is already low, \(1/M\) is the right default and the flatter proposals are held in reserve.
crumbs: 11  Pipeline B in Practice: a Tempering Experiment

title: 11  Pipeline B in Practice: a Tempering Experiment
text:
11.3 The honest scope
This is one regime, and a benign one — the bridge happened to be a good proposal at \(1/M\). Your concern was about the other case: large \(n\), well-separated per-PV “islands,” where \(\hat k\) at \(1/M\) breaches the gate. We no longer have to leave that to expectation: the controlled experiment of Section 10.1 reproduces it — at a simulated PISA scale (\(J=150\)) the \(1/M\) bridge’s worst \(\hat k\) climbs to \(0.83\), past the \(0.7\) gate, and Pipeline B does the safe thing, refusing to emit and falling back to C-Direct. The islands case is no longer hypothetical: the gate is shown to fire, and B to fail safe, on our own data.
The honest scope has two halves, then. When the bridge breaks — islands — the gate catches it and C-Direct stands alone. When the bridge holds — as on the real PISA reading data, where \(\hat k\le0.07\) (Section 13.1) — B’s point agrees but its pooled standard errors still run below the orthodox target, by up to a third for the between-school slope, because B reweights one shared draw cloud. Either way the conclusion is the one your comment pointed toward: C-Direct is the reported default — not a patch over a broken B, but the deliverable that never builds importance weights in the first place — with B’s \(\hat k\) the diagnostic that tells you which regime you are in, and the tempering and two-MCMC levers of Section 7.1 ready for the harder data.
crumbs: 11  Pipeline B in Practice: a Tempering Experiment

title: 12  PISA 2022: Data and Model Setup
text:
12.1 From simulation to the real thing
The simulation showed the three pipelines agree in point where we control the truth — with C-Direct reproducing the orthodox standard errors exactly. The remaining question is whether that holds on the actual PISA 2022 data, with its real plausible values, real survey weights, and real replicate-weight design. This chapter fixes the data and model; Section 13.1 reports the run.
crumbs: 12  PISA 2022: Data and Model Setup

title: 12  PISA 2022: Data and Model Setup
text:

We use PISA 2022 for the United States and Korea, drawn from the public-use files and prepared in code/04_pisa_prep.R. After listwise deletion on the analysis variables, the analytic samples are:

Two honest notes on scope. First, this demonstration uses reading achievement (the EST-01 specification that was already prepared and validated in the project’s data pipeline); re-extracting the mathematics plausible values from the raw files would not change anything about the pipeline comparison, which is the point here. Second — and relatedly — the reading ICCs are similar across the two countries (0.19 and 0.22), rather than the sharp 0.20-vs-0.45 contrast the proposal cited, which is a feature of mathematics (where Korea’s between-school variance is much larger). The pipeline-agreement question does not depend on a large ICC contrast, so reading serves it well; the substantive cross-country story is a separate matter, addressed below.
crumbs: 12  PISA 2022: Data and Model Setup

title: 12  PISA 2022: Data and Model Setup
text:
12.3 The model and the estimand
The analyst’s model is the two-level random-intercept hybrid of Section 3.1, here with a single covariate — the economic, social and cultural status index (ESCS) — entered in within/between form: \[
\text{READ}^{(m)}_{ij} = \beta_0 + \beta^W(\text{ESCS}_{ij}-\overline{\text{ESCS}}_{\cdot j})
                        + \beta^B\,\overline{\text{ESCS}}_{\cdot j} + \zeta_j + \varepsilon^{(m)}_{ij}.
\] The two estimands are the within-school SES gradient \(\beta^W\) (how a student’s relative advantage predicts their score) and the between-school gradient \(\beta^B\) (how a school’s average advantage predicts its scores) — the latter the larger, more policy-salient quantity, and the one with more missing-information sensitivity (Section 4.1).
crumbs: 12  PISA 2022: Data and Model Setup

title: 12  PISA 2022: Data and Model Setup
section: 12.4 What we run, and on which weights
text:
12.4 What we run, and on which weights
All three pipelines run on each country:

Pipeline A assembles the BRR-Fay target with the real assemble_pisa_brr_fay_target — \(M(R{+}1)=810\) survey-weighted lme4 fits per country — and Rubin-pools (the reference).
Pipeline C-Direct fits the stacked fractional bridge once in brms and calibrates with ccc_twolevel against that \(T_{\text{MI}}\).
Pipeline B reweights the same single fit per PV by PSIS and reports the Pareto \(\hat k\).

The student weight W_FSTUWT and the 80 replicate weights drive the design-based variance. A note on the weighting, because it matters for honesty: the substantive headline of the parent paper — that the USA–Korea between-school SES gap narrows and loses significance under the exact two-stage PISA weights (school weight times conditional student weight) — is a separate contribution, and we do not claim it here. This chapter’s question is narrower and methodological: do the three pipelines produce the same numbers on real data? That comparison — whether A, B, and C-Direct agree — holds for any fixed weighting, since all three share it; the estimates themselves of course depend on the weights, which is exactly why the parent paper’s exact-two-stage-weight story is a separate contribution. We use the standard final and replicate weights throughout and let that substantive weighting debate live in the paper proper.
Section 13.1 has the answer.
crumbs: 12  PISA 2022: Data and Model Setup

title: 13  PISA 2022: Three Pipelines, Side by Side
text:
13.1 The answer on real data
Here are all three pipelines, run on PISA 2022 reading for the USA and Korea. The estimands are the SES gradients \(\beta^W\) (within-school) and \(\beta^B\) (between-school), on the PISA score scale.
Three things to read off this.
Pipeline C-Direct reproduces Pipeline A exactly — point and standard error. This is the central result, and on real data it is now exact in both coordinates. Because Pipeline C-Direct is centered at the external \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) that Pipeline A reports and its covariance is the Cholesky-calibrated reshaping of the real stacked draws onto the very \(T_{\text{MI}}\) A assembles by Rubin pooling, the two pipelines return the same numbers: \[
\max\bigl|\hat\beta_C-\hat\beta_A\bigr| = 0\ \text{(point, exact)}, \qquad
\max\bigl|\text{SE}_C-\text{SE}_A\bigr| = 2.7\times10^{-15}\ (\text{USA}),\ \ 2.2\times10^{-15}\ (\text{Korea}).
\] These standard errors come from the real pipeline_c_direct calibrating the real brms draws — there is no synthetic draw cloud anywhere in the computation. The one-MCMC report carries the orthodox ten-fit report’s uncertainty by construction, not by luck; the simulation (Section 10.1) showed this on known-truth data, and here it holds on PISA. The raw stacked posterior mean does sit a short distance from \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) — \(0.33\) (USA) and \(0.43\) (Korea), the R3a→R3b gap of Section 3.1 — but that gap is a verification diagnostic; it is not carried into C-Direct’s report, which is centered at \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) exactly.
Pipeline B agrees in point, and its \(\hat k\) is tiny — but read its standard errors carefully. The worst per-PV Pareto \(\hat k\) is \(0.07\) (USA) and \(0.04\) (Korea), far inside the green tier: the importance sampling you asked us to scrutinize (C1) is stable on these data without any tempering, exactly as Section 11.1 anticipated, and B’s point estimates track A and C-Direct to within a few tenths. (In the controlled experiment of Section 10.1 the same gate fires at \(\hat k=0.83\) on a simulated \(J=150\) design — a reminder that it can and does breach; the real reading data here simply does not stress the proposal that hard.) Its standard errors, however, run below the orthodox reference — most visibly for the between-school slope, where B reports \(2.29\) vs A’s \(2.89\) for the USA and \(2.08\) vs \(3.15\) for Korea, intervals up to a third narrower. The mechanism is structural: Pipeline B’s \(M\) per-PV estimates are reweightings of a single shared draw cloud, so they are correlated, and their between-imputation spread is mechanically smaller than the spread of \(M\) independently refit models — the \(O_p(S^{-1/2})\) reuse of one proposal that Section 7.1 flagged. (Part of the gap is also that Pipeline A’s ten-refit \(B\) is itself a noisy estimate of the target, so this is estimator-against-estimator, not against truth; but the direction is clear, and B’s narrower intervals are anti-conservative — too short, with an under-coverage risk.) The lesson is sharp: a small \(\hat k\) certifies that the importance sampling is stable, not that B’s multiple-imputation variance reproduces the orthodox target \(T_{\text{MI}}\). This is precisely why the recommended default is C-Direct — which targets \(T_{\text{MI}}\) directly and so matches A exactly — with B kept as the diagnostic that flags when the stacked proposal is in trouble. The tempering and two-MCMC levers (Section 11.1) stay in reserve for data that stress the proposal harder.

title: 13  PISA 2022: Three Pipelines, Side by Side
text:
13.2 What it cost
Pipeline A required ten fits’ worth of work; Pipelines B and C-Direct required one stacked MCMC each, sharing it. The BRR-Fay target — the object your comments C2 and C4 asked about — was assembled in about ten seconds per country from the 810 fast lme4 refits, and is shared by all three. The single stacked brms fit was the real cost (about ten minutes for the USA and twenty-six for Korea at this \(43{,}000\)–\(64{,}000\)-row scale, a pilot-grade setting); ten such fits, the orthodox path, would have been hours. The economy is the MCMC count, one against ten — precisely the claim of Section 5.1, now visible on real data.

title: 13  PISA 2022: Three Pipelines, Side by Side
section: 13.3 An honest note on the substance
text:
13.3 An honest note on the substance
These numbers reproduce the project’s established reading estimates (\(\beta^W\approx25\) for the USA, \(\approx28\) for Korea), which is reassurance that the pipeline is computing the right thing. They are not, however, the parent paper’s substantive headline: that story concerns what happens to the cross-country between-school gap under the exact two-stage PISA weights, and it is a separate analysis. What this chapter establishes is only — and exactly — what it set out to: on real PISA data, the per-PV orthodox pipeline, the stacked-PSIS pipeline, and the stacked-direct pipeline give the same point answer — C-Direct reproducing the orthodox intervals exactly, the stacked-PSIS pipeline’s running a touch narrower — and the last of them does it with a single MCMC. The methodological claim of the proposal holds on the data it was written for.

title: 14  Response to Your Comments
text:
14.1 Closing the loop
This book opened with your five comments and a promise to answer each one from four angles — mathematics, code, a known-truth simulation, and PISA itself. Here is the ledger, closed.
You were right — and the saving is narrower than the proposal made it sound. \(T_{\text{MI}}\) is model-specific and it does take many fits. But they are \(M(R{+}1)=810\) fast lme4 REML refits, not MCMC runs, assembled by a pipeline-independent BRR-Fay subroutine that all three pipelines share (Section 5.1). In code it is one function, assemble_pisa_brr_fay_target (Section 8.1); on the real PISA data it ran in about ten seconds per country (Section 13.1). Pipeline C does not get the variance target “without A or B” in the sense of getting it for free — it gets it from the same cheap machinery everyone uses, and its only economy is the MCMC count, ten down to one. And the target is not just algebra: in a known-truth simulation with informative, unequal-probability sampling, the BRR-Fay \(T_{\text{MI}}\) delivers nominal interval coverage where ignoring the design collapses to \(0.07\) (Section 10.8) — so it is design-valid, not merely model-valid. (That check stresses within-school informative selection; informative school selection — where the exact two-stage weights bite hardest — is the natural next stress-test, flagged rather than claimed.)
It is a variance of the betas, not of \(y\). Each plausible value is pushed through the GLS map \(\hat\beta_m=(X^\top\Omega X)^{-1}X^\top\Omega\,y^{(m)}\), and \(B\) is the covariance of those ten coefficient vectors in parameter space — the missing arrow, drawn explicitly via the sandwich \(\operatorname{Cov}(\hat\beta_m)=F\operatorname{Cov}(y^{(m)})F^\top\) (Section 4.1). The variability originates in \(y\) but is measured in \(\beta\).
Two answers, because it is two questions. Why only the betas: the licensing identity is exact only for the fixed effects (their GLS gradient is linear in \(y\)), there is no design-based target for the variance components in this paper, and an affine map would violate their positivity — so calibrating them is unsafe, not merely unmotivated (Section 6.1). And the simulation made the boundary visible: the fixed-effect identity held to \(10^{-14}\) while the variance-component “identity” drifted \(4.7\%\) at the pilot scale and \(0.7\%\) at PISA scale (Section 10.1). Why not joint resampling: that is Pipeline B’s PSIS for the \(\beta\) block (Section 7.1) — joint variance-component resampling walks straight into the heavy-tail problem you flag in C1, which is why we keep it for the follow-up paper.
We built your ideas and ran them. The Pareto \(\hat k\) gate is in place; the tempering exponent \(1/(kM)\) is a real lever; the two-MCMC rescue is specified (Section 7.1). The experiment of Section 11.1 ran \(1/M\) vs \(1/2M\) vs \(1/3M\) on known-truth data: at \(1/M\) the worst \(\hat k\) was \(0.25\) (already reliable), tempering pushed it lower still (your mechanism, confirmed), and the point estimate barely moved — vindicating your instinct to run \(1/M\) first. On the real PISA data the worst \(\hat k\) was \(0.07\) (USA) and \(0.04\) (Korea) (Section 13.1): the importance sampling is stable there, and Pipeline B’s point tracks A and C-Direct. Its standard errors, though, run a little below the orthodox target — most for the between-school slope — because B reweights a single shared draw cloud; a small \(\hat k\) certifies that the sampler is stable, not that B’s multiple-imputation variance equals A’s. So C-Direct is the reported deliverable, with B the diagnostic alongside it, and the tempering and second-fit levers in reserve for harder data.

And the three results that, together, are the whole argument:

Theorem 2.2 is exact: the stacked GLS solution equalled the Rubin estimate to \(10^{-14}\) (Section 10.1).
Pipeline C’s point and uncertainty are Pipeline A’s, exactly: centered at the external \(\bar\beta_{\text{Rubin}}\) and calibrated to the same \(T_{\text{MI}}\), C-Direct reproduces A’s point (\(\max|\hat\beta_C-\hat\beta_A|=0\)) and its standard errors (\(\max|\text{SE}_C-\text{SE}_A|\) is \(0\) to floating point, \(\le 3\times10^{-15}\) on PISA) — in simulation and on real data.
It holds on the real data: USA and Korea, the three pipelines agree in point — C-Direct reproduces A’s intervals exactly, while B’s run narrower (by up to a third for the between-school slope) — at \(\hat k=0.04\)–\(0.07\), with one MCMC instead of ten.

title: 14  Response to Your Comments
section: 14.3 Where I’d like to go next
text:
14.3 Where I’d like to go next
A few things I’d value your steer on:

The tempering follow-up. We now have a first reproduction of the case you sketched: at a simulated PISA scale (\(J=150\)) the \(1/M\) bridge’s worst \(\hat k\) climbs to \(0.83\), past the gate, and Pipeline B fails safe to C-Direct (Section 10.1) — the large-\(n\), separated per-PV “islands” you anticipated, exhibited rather than asserted. I’d like to develop it into a fuller study with the second-fit rescue earning its keep. This feels like a paper-worthy methodological contribution in its own right.
The exact two-stage weighting. The substantive PISA story (the between-school gap under exact school-times-conditional-student weights) sits alongside this methodology and is where the empirical headline lives; I’d like to align the pipeline machinery with that weighting next.
Overleaf. Thank you for offering to host — yes, let’s. I’ll port the manuscript skeleton there so the comments and edits flow both ways.

Thank you for comments precise enough to organize a whole book around. If any answer here still doesn’t land, that is the most useful thing you could tell me.
— JoonHo
